230348.explanation: On the contrary: the Party for the Animals wants to discourage large-scale ‘prestige’ events, especially if they are only intended to put Maastricht on the map. Our vision for the cultural budget is to support accessible cultural activities, at local level especially.

230349.explanation: Quality education helps children develop good social, emotional, motor, and creative skills. The Party for the Animals therefore wishes to invest in sports and swimming lessons in schools. We want to make sports and swimming accessible to all children, regardless of their socio-economic background.

230350.explanation: Currently, extra tutoring is often only accessible to those who can afford it. The Party for the Animals wants to make training and homework assistance at schools accessible to all, thereby offering all children equal opportunities.

230353.explanation: Capacity shortages have led to a lack of enforcement on animal rights and environmental issues in Maastricht. The Party for the Animals therefore wishes to increase the number of BOAs. We also want the Maastricht enforcement team to be a representative cross-section of the community.

230354.explanation: Streets are for everyone, which is why we want strict enforcement of freely accessible footpaths and guidance strips. We also want to ensure enough free bicycle parking.

230355.explanation: Cameras provide the illusion of safety, and only displace the problem. The PvdD therefore advocates a more conservative use of video surveillance in public spaces: as a temporary measure, and subject to strict conditions. We prefer to tackle the problem at the source.

230356.explanation: Though not entirely in favour of more student housing in neighbourhoods, the Party for the Animals would like a better distribution of housing types across the city. Every street should offer something for everyone, to prevent segregation and to encourage a sense of community among different Maastricht population groups.

230357.explanation: Tourism causes significant damage to the climate and the environment through travel emissions and overconsumption, and also impacts liveability for residents. The PvdD wants a ban on both new hotels and on replacements of hotels that close, until a considerable reduction in tourism has been achieved.

230358.explanation: Currently, where we are parking cars we could be planting greenery and letting our children play. The Party for the Animals wants to concentrate on introducing park-and-ride facilities on the outskirts of residential areas, encouraging cycling and public transport, limiting parking in new housing, and incorporating indoor parking into high-rise buildings.

230359.explanation: Houses are for living in, not making a profit. The PvdD wants a fair blend of housing categories in every construction project, as well as plans for genuinely affordable homes for first-time buyers or people on a limited budget.

230360.explanation: Proper housing is a basic right. To help enable young people to continue living in their own city, the Party for the Animals advocates for increased mobility and access to suitable housing, more social housing (40%), and a halt to the demolition and privatisation of social rental homes.

230361.explanation: Before ramping up construction in Maastricht, the PvdD wants to see more efficient use of existing buildings. Only after that will we look at densification, coupled with additional greenery. Building at the city’s edges will be a last resort, only where the environment permits, and where there is no detriment to nature or liveability.

230362.explanation: The Party for the Animals advocates increasing the number of mobile home sites, and calls on the municipality to actively support finding locations where residents, travellers, Roma, and Sinti can maintain their traditional way of life.

230364.explanation: Investments in debt assistance are investments in health. The PvdD wants to allow the municipality to buy off debts under strict conditions. This is ultimately cheaper for society, and allows those in debt to spend their energy on building a future.

230365.explanation: The PvdD is against mutual participation requirements for people entitled to welfare benefits. Not only is it undesirable for people to work without pay, it also displaces regular paid employment. Welfare recipients need the chance to focus on talent development, and working for pay.

230366.explanation: In Maastricht, everybody should be able to take part in society, regardless of their financial situation. The PvdD wants to support people on incomes up to 130% of the social minimum, including help with sports, culture, and veterinary care costs. To combat stigmatisation, all residents should receive a pass.

230368.explanation: The sale of rental social housing impedes mobility. The PvdD is therefore against the sale of these homes, unless they are replaced in equal numbers – there is a shortage of social housing, after all. We will also focus on the effective distribution of social rentals within each neighbourhood.

230369.explanation: On the contrary, the PvdD wants less parking in the city centre. Visitors should be encouraged to cycle or take public transport. We do support the creation of P+R facilities at the edge of town, however, where visitors can park their car and continue into town on public transport.

230370.explanation: To improve liveability and safety, the PvdD wants a speed limit of 30 km/h on streets without a dedicated bicycle path. Streets without sidewalks should be converted into shared residential areas, with a speed limit of 15 km/h. The same should apply to schools and childcare facilities.

230371.explanation: Cycling is healthy, and better for the environment. The PvdD therefore supports more safe bicycle routes, to encourage cycling as an attractive alternative to going by car. New bike routes should not impact nature, however, or the safety of humans or animals.

230372.explanation: The PvdD aims to transform parking spaces in key areas into wider sidewalks, playgrounds, flowered verges, small parks, or bike parking facilities, making the city more liveable. To do so we will invest in shared mobility, park+ride options, and encourage cycling and public transport.

230373.explanation: The train is an effective and environmentally friendly alternative to car transport. The PvdD therefore wants Maastricht to invest in a train to Hasselt, and join forces with the province to encourage international train connectivity.

230374.explanation: Paid parking should be implemented throughout the city. By doing so, the Party for the Animals aims to reduce parking congestion, discourage the use of cars, and promote other travel alternatives. Universal paid parking will also stop drivers migrating to ‘free’ neighbourhoods.

230375.explanation: Air quality in Maastricht is poor, especially in the city centre. Fewer cars means less air pollution, and better health. The Party for the Animals therefore advocates a car-free inner city where possible.

230376.explanation: Mental health problems among young people are clearly on the rise, and youth suicide continues to increase. The PvdD therefore supports raising the budget for youth mental health services. Young people need sufficient access to psychological help.

230378.explanation: Informal carers are essential to a caring society. The PvdD therefore wishes to offer better support to informal caregivers, and  financial compensation to prevent burnout. We advocate flexible study for young carers, and paid informal care leave.

230380.explanation: Not everybody has access to reusable or other menstruation products, which can be a major impediment to living life. The PvdD believes that these products should be available at public locations for people who cannot afford them, who have forgotten them, or who have irregular or unexpected periods.

230320.explanation: A lobby register makes politics more transparent and fairer. Citizens should be able to see which actors meet with local executives, so it is clear who exercises influence on the municipal administration.

230321.explanation: Sometimes external experts are needed for complex policy dossiers, but this must be efficient, transparent and not used too easily. At present such consultancies are deployed too quickly.

230322.explanation: At present, we do not yet see the participatory budget as an effective form of citizen participation. It would need to be far more representative, similar to a citizens’ assembly. If that were the case, we would support allocating more funding to the participatory budget.

230323.explanation: A good alderman, executive or mayor already frequently visits citizens, employers and employees throughout the city. A structural placement for civil servants and executives would make this more consistent. This would help Maastricht politics to better understand what other citizens experience.

230324.explanation: Volt supports (p)referendums only under strict conditions, but prefers deliberative democracy. Citizens, guided by experts, issue binding recommendations in a citizens' assembly. With a permanent citizens' assembly, referendums become unnecessary, as issues would then be handled within the assembly.

230325.explanation: In an uncertain world, Europe must invest in its industries. The EU isn'tt an abstract office in Brussels. Europe is Maastricht, too. Investing in Brightlands strengthens education, innovation and our regional position. As a hub between countries, Maastricht can attract more talent and activity.

230326.explanation: Volt wants to slightly increase the property tax (OZB) to the level of similar municipalities, but only if this capital tax is used to build new homes. A home-for-home tax, in other words. This yields 7 million euros annually to build affordable housing and to purchase necessary land.

230327.explanation: We want to increase the tourist tax to the level of other tourist cities in the Netherlands. When tourists contribute to the services they use, the revenues can be used for sport, culture and liveability in residential neighbourhoods.

230328.explanation: Volt aims for a city centre that is more walkable and bicycle-friendly. This has no negative consequences for entrepreneurs. In fact, examples such as Belgium's Ghent and Italy's Florence show that a more liveable centre is a benefit for entrepreneurs, including for those in residential areas.

230329.explanation: To keep our city safe and clean, the municipality must spend money: on cleaning staff, enforcement and other facilities. One way to cover these costs is through the dog taxation.

230330.explanation: Maastricht should no longer spend money on tourism promotion. Instead, this money should be used to attract and retain talent and economic activity. Maastricht is ageing rapidly and this will soon become a problem for our local society.

230331.explanation: Volt supports longer opening hours for supermarkets, bakeries and similar essential shops. At present we already attract many day tourists from neighbouring countries. Clothing shops and other non-essential shops, therefore, do not need to be open that late on Sundays.

230332.explanation: Volt supports the current Sunday shopping policy. Non-essential shops (such as clothing shops) open at 12:00 and close at 18:00 on Sundays. This is beneficial because our city cannot handle even more day tourism from neighbouring countries (where Sunday shopping is less common).

230333.explanation: This is a fictional debate: the rights of status holders is legally anchored in the Netherlands. Maastricht can't change this. Research also shows that the housing shortage is caused by years of political inaction in housing. Ending this priority locally is impossible and has little effect.

230334.explanation: Too many students leave after graduation. Many say they lack opportunities to learn the language and to meet employers in the cross-border region (the Euregion). With good language education and Euregional cooperation, the municipality, UM and Zuyd should develop a joint talent retention strategy.

230335.explanation: Language skills help residents participate in our cross-border region. This benefits social cohesion and the labour market. Volt wants to offer free Dutch courses to international residents, as well as French and German for cross-border workers, and Limburgish for other newcomers.

230336.explanation: Maastricht must contribute in European solidarity and guarantee a humane reception of refugees. Where possible, we want more refugees to be hosted in wealthy neighbourhoods. Europe must remain a home for people fleeing war.

230337.explanation: It is currently more appealing for people to buy a second-hand bicycle than to collect their bicycle from the depot. As a result, many bicycles remain at the depot. The fine must cover municipal costs and not be disproportionally high.

230338.explanation: Alcohol policy enforcement must act proportionally, professionally and respectfully. Stricter enforcement is only possible when there are concrete violations and risks. The municipality must also engage more actively with associations to emphasise prevention of alcohol abuse.

230339.explanation: Cultural free spaces strengthen creativity, social interaction, a vibrant young city, housing and cultural experimentation. This belongs to a lively and diverse cultural city with a young community.

230340.explanation: Nature-inclusive construction helps prevent heat, flooding and loss of biodiversity. It makes neighbourhoods more liveable without using additional scarce space. Volt wants Maastricht to develop an ambitious green vision with its Belgian border municipalities (Lanaken, Visé) for the border region.

230341.explanation: Reducing pesticides around homes protects people's health and the environment. The municipality should go beyond the national minimum.

230342.explanation: Trees are crucial against heat, flooding and bad air quality. Therefore, tree-cutting rules must become stricter and greening should become the norm. Volt also wants Maastricht to set up a more ambitious green vision with its Belgian border municipalities (Lanaken and Visé) for the border region.

230343.explanation: Volt sees little value for today's Maastricht in investing additional public funds to attract even more day tourists (i.e., short-stay tourists). Instead, this money should be used to attract and retain (international) talent, students and economic activity.

230346.explanation: Sports facilities are important for health and social interaction. With growing demand, expansion must be explored. Our election programme explains how we will rapidly finance such facilities in the short term.

230347.explanation: Volt wants the sport and culture budget to grow substantially short-term and later be doubled. Sport and culture support youth, sometimes more effectively than traditional youth care. A strong cultural policy also attracts young talent. This makes Maastricht a lively, young and accessible cultural.

230348.explanation: It depends on the type of event. We oppose additional Vrijthof events that bring even more tourism. We want more support for neighbourhood events and more conferences that connect knowledge and talent in the cross-border region, such as an annual Smart Meuse-Rhine conference with Liège and Aachen.

230349.explanation: Swimming skills are essential for health and safety in a city that is surrounded by so much water. Not all families can afford swimming classes. School swimming should therefore be available for everyone.

230350.explanation: Not all children receive the same support at home. Investing in tutoring and homework support increases equal opportunities. This is even more important in a city like Maastricht, where inequality is very high.

230353.explanation: Well-trained municipal enforcement officers ensure safety and understanding in neighbourhoods. Volt wants to invest in professional, inclusive and accessible enforcement that operates preventively and builds trust. Safety for women at night is a major priority for Volt.

230354.explanation: Incorrectly parked bicycles hinder pedestrians and especially people who are physically challenged. Bicycle enforcement is need for an accessible cycling city, but must go hand in hand with more bicycle parking facilities and the reduction of on-street parking spaces for tourists in the city centre.

230355.explanation: Camera surveillance must be proportionate and targeted. Safety in the city, for example for women during the night-time, is very important. Although cameras are not always the solution, reducing surveillance is not the solution to a safer city for women and other residents.

230356.explanation: Student rooms fall under rental, splitting and liveability regulations. Municipalities may not exclude people based on identity (e.g., “student”). Volt wants more housing campuses, for example in Randwyck, but doesn't see exclusion of student housing in neighbourhoods as part of a solution.

230357.explanation: Maastricht currently has enough hotels. Volt wants the municipality to acquire and own more land, so that housing is prioritised over hotels. Our scarce space should not be left to the free market.

230358.explanation: We want fewer on-street parking spaces for tourists. If a neighbourhood has sufficient parking for residents, we remove the remaining ones. These are replaced with greenery to reduce heat stress.

230359.explanation: The housing market must remain affordable for starters and lower and middle incomes. Volt therefore wants the majority of new developments to consist of affordable rental, affordable owner-occupied housing and state-regulated social housing.

230360.explanation: Social housing's waiting lists already prioritise according to registration duration: these people are mostly people with ties to Maastricht. The housing shortage stems from years of insufficient social housing construction; changing the priority-system has little effect and is legally problematic.

230361.explanation: Volt mainly wants inner-city development, such as mid and high rise in modern districts like Randwyck, tunnelling the railway, and densifying existing areas. Expansion to the north is possible, but Volt strongly opposes construction behind the Molenweg and other areas where nature would be harmed.

230362.explanation: Travellers, nomadic people and trailer inhabitants have the right to adequate housing. More housing is needed to address current backlogs. This is in line with the rights of travellers under European law and the EU's fundamental human rights.

230364.explanation: Paying off debts as the government can help people to escape hopeless situations. This has to be done under strict conditions. Such policies increase the chance of a fresh start, while leaving behind long-term poverty. We should learn from successful examples in Limburg and elsewhere in Europe.

230365.explanation: Participation must be stimulated through training and guidance, not through coercion/punishment. Volt wants people to participate, not perform mandatory unpaid work. Evaluations from municipalities that have forced people to perform unpaid work show limited effectiveness and negative side effects.

230366.explanation: Everyone must be able to participate in sport and culture, including those with low incomes. A social card or social pass lowers barriers and strengthens cohesion. Based on successful social cards in The Hague and Brussels, Volt proposes a Maastricht social pass.

230368.explanation: The Vienna model shows how important municipal control is in addressing the housing crisis. Selling social housing reduces the affordable stock. Volt wants to expand the number of social housing and, instead, wants to support starters through other routes, rather than shrinking social housing.

230369.explanation: Volt chooses a low-car city centre with more space for green zones, for pedestrians and for cyclists. Tourists should park at Park and Ride zones on the edges of the city, which creates more parking space in the centre for residents, workers and health care, without adding new parking spaces.

230370.explanation: A lower maximum speed makes streets safer for pedestrians and cyclists. This improves liveability in neighbourhoods. It also makes rules more coherent if almost the whole city is 30 km/h, except where this would be unsafe, such as the city ring roads (i.e., the "Singels")

230371.explanation: Safe cycling routes connect neighbourhoods and make the city more accessible, especially for children and older residents. More bike routes to schools and walking routes to bus stops are crucial. We also want more cycling routes to Belgium. This stimulates healthy mobility and reduces car traffic.

230372.explanation: On-street parking takes up significant space. Redirecting tourist traffic to the edge of the city allows parking in the city centre to be reduced. Removing parking spaces frees room for green zones, wider pavements, bicycle parking and new meeting spaces.

230373.explanation: A better train connection with Hasselt and Belgium strengthens the region. This benefits accessibility, the economy and education. It is important to renew the current railway bridge and restore a Maastricht connection with Flanders, while investing in more connections with Liège and Aachen.

230374.explanation: Volt wants parking policy tailored per neighbourhood, based on liveability and accessibility. Some neighbourhoods may wish to increase visitor parking fees while others may not. A general ban on parking fee expansion does not fit this approach.

230375.explanation: Volt wants tourist car traffic redirected to P+R facilities at the city’s edges. Streets should become car-free where possible, accessible only to residents, workers, entrepreneurs and caregivers.

230376.explanation: Young people experience increasing psychological pressure. Investing in mental health prevents larger problems at a later moment. This requires structural funding for youth care and education, but also for sport and culture, as it has been demonstrated that they support (mental) health.

230378.explanation: Informal carers, family carers, and other care give play a major role in caring for older and ill residents. Extra support for them prevents overload and burnout.

230380.explanation: Access to menstrual products is a basic necessity.

230320.explanation: In Maastricht, property developers, lobbying groups and major businesses have no trouble finding their way to municipal executive members, often behind closed doors. The result is back-door politics, which undermines trust in fair decisions. The SP wants maximum transparency.

230321.explanation: The SP believes that costly consultancy firms are used too often, while in-house knowledge needs development. Investing in our own employees makes policy less expensive, more transparent, and better tailored to the needs of the city.

230322.explanation: The citizen budget is a sham: it is the outspoken and well-organised residents who benefit most, and the application process is needlessly complicated. The SP therefore favours neighbourhood budgets, that allow residents to decide where their own money goes. They know best what their neighbourhoods need.

230323.explanation: The SP believes that those who make policy should also know their city. We therefore support mandatory civic placements: civil servants and administrators would spend part of their working hours gaining hands-on experience at locations such as community centres, food banks, or in the care sector, helping to reduce the gap between city hall and the city itself.

230324.explanation: On genuinely serious issues, Maastricht residents themselves must have the final say. The SP therefore supports an accessible referendum as a democratic handbrake. And if the results are clear, the council cannot simply brush it aside, but must take their plans back to the drawing board.

230325.explanation: Brightlands needs to stand on its own two feet: innovation is a positive thing, but not at the expense of liveability and opportunities for all Maastricht residents. Quickly make the campus financially independent, listen to ordinary residents, and let their voices guide the creation of a greener, more liveable neighbourhood.

230326.explanation: The OZB does not necessarily need to be higher. In the SP’s view, it need only be indexed once a year. Only when Maastricht faces tough decisions – such as the closure of community centres or cutting service budgets – should a temporary increase be considered.

230327.explanation: Tourism provides revenue for some sectors, but ordinary Maastricht residents are saddled with the costs (crowding, nuisance, cleaning). A higher tourist tax means that visitors make a fair contribution to the liveability and maintenance of our city, and also enables investments in our neighbourhoods.

230328.explanation: This is a factual impossibility, so we cannot formulate a position on it. Any party who claims that they can is duping voters, plain and simple.

230329.explanation: The SP believes the dog tax is unfair, and no longer belongs in this day and age. To many people, dogs are a friend and a source of essential support in the fight against loneliness – not a luxury item to be taxed.

230330.explanation: The SP does not see the value in promotional speeches for businesses. All too often, money just disappears into marketing slogans, providing little benefit for the average resident. A better investment would be in affordable homes, good jobs, resilient neighbourhoods, and public services – then businesses will follow automatically.

230331.explanation: The SP wants to preserve Sunday peace and quiet as much as possible. Sundays are for ourselves, our family and friends, but also things like football clubs and other associations that keep Maastricht life going. Not every hour in the week has to be dedicated to shopping and turning a profit.

230332.explanation: We want to preserve Sunday peace and quiet as much as possible. Sundays are for ourselves, our family and friends, but also football clubs and other associations that keep Maastricht life going. Not every hour in the week has to be dedicated to consumerism and turning a profit.

230333.explanation: Denying recognised refugees priority will not solve the housing shortage, and anybody who says otherwise is lying. They are not the problem, and by pursuing this policy, families are ending up on the street. The real problem is that for decades, too little social housing has been built for all of us.

230334.explanation: The SP wants a city where we can live together instead of passing each other by. International students are not outsiders, but people who live, work, and study here. We therefore wish to join forces with the university for genuine integration: active participation in neighbourhoods, associations, and volunteer work.

230335.explanation: Free language classes for international residents are not a municipal responsibility. Universities and employers already offer these to their students and employees. This funding could be better spent on housing, healthcare, and services that will directly benefit all Maastricht residents.

230336.explanation: Maastricht already hosts more asylum seekers than required, and generally there have been few problems. But to accommodate even more is not fair, when other municipalities are not stepping up. We want both fair distribution and high-quality, human-centred accommodation that is sustainable for everybody.

230337.explanation: Fifty euro is simply exorbitant for parking a bike incorrectly. For many in Maastricht, a bicycle is no luxury, but a necessity for getting to work, school, or healthcare. 15 euro remains a deterrent and is enforceable, without hurting those on limited budgets.

230338.explanation: Associations run on volunteers and engaged parents, not on BOAs and extra regulations. The SP wants to support sports clubs and community centres, not saddle them with more supervision and administration. Raising children is first and foremost the responsibility of parents, not of volunteers behind the bar.

230339.explanation: Cultural free spaces such as the Landbouwbelang are essential for a lively city. They provide space for experimentation, social contact, and alternative culture. The SP has been committed to their protection for years: no eviction without a real alternative and guaranteed space for free cultural expression.

230340.explanation: Nature-inclusive construction is no luxury, but a real necessity. It helps combat heat stress, creates cooler neighbourhoods, and improves health and well-being. Protecting quality homes against heat makes neighbourhoods more liveable, especially for those without a garden or air conditioning.

230341.explanation: The health of our residents takes precedence over the interests of agriculture. The SP wants to expand pesticide-free zones around homes to at least 250 metres, protecting people against toxic chemicals and creating a safe and healthy living environment – for children and other vulnerable groups especially.

230342.explanation: Trees are fundamental and indispensable: they keep our streets cooler, help with drainage, and make the air healthier to breathe. The SP therefore wants stricter regulations surrounding the felling and planting of trees. In concrete-heavy neighbourhoods especially, trees represent pure health benefits for us all.

230343.explanation: Maastricht has no shortage of tourists – what we lack is liveability. The SP wants to end subsidies for the city marketing industry (such as Maastricht Marketing and Centrummanagement), which mainly serve the interests of branding and chain businesses. That funding could be going to neighbourhoods and local businesses: that’s where the real economy is.

230346.explanation: The Geusseltbad pool is a fundamental public facility. Expanding it will offer more space and longer opening hours for free school swimming lessons, recreational swimming, seniors, social support users, and toddlers. Swimming is healthy, safe and should be accessible to all, not just to those who can afford it.

230347.explanation: Youth culture is no by-product, but an essential part of the city. Those with money can always find somewhere to go, but people on a budget are now often left out. The SP wants affordable and accessible opportunities for all young people in Maastricht to create, rehearse, and perform  in their own neighbourhood.

230348.explanation: Major events are often unaffordable to the average Maastricht resident, and create crowding and nuisance in the city. The SP prefers small-scale, accessible events and folk culture, at neighbourhood level for everybody. This promotes liveability and genuine connections in the city.

230349.explanation: Extra funding is welcome, but the SP would like to go a lot further, and make school swimming lessons completely free for all Maastricht students. Swimming is not a luxury or ‘bonus’ for children, but an essential life skill that should not be dependent on their parents’ income.

230350.explanation: The SP wants more support for training and homework assistance, especially for children and families who are struggling the most. Not indiscriminate funding, but targeted investment where there is hardship. This genuinely creates equal opportunities, instead of just talking the talk.

230353.explanation: More BOAs means more street presence and faster intervention where there are problems. They are the eyes and ears of our neighbourhoods: against incorrectly parked bicycles, illegal dumping of waste, and unlawful rental practices. They should, however, receive a much higher salary, and safe, proper conditions of employment.

230354.explanation: Incorrectly parked bicycles block sidewalks, streets, and entrances, often literally preventing senior citizens, wheelchair users, and those with prams or walking frames from getting past. The SP therefore wants stricter monitoring and enforcement, to ensure accessibility of public spaces for everybody.

230355.explanation: The SP wants a safe city, but not one with non-stop surveillance. Security cameras are a harsh measure, as they intrude on our privacy and have not always been proven effective. The SP prefers street presence: more BOAs and a targeted neighbourhood approach, rather than more video surveillance.

230356.explanation: Students are a part of Maastricht, but neighbourhoods must remain liveable. Excessive subdivision erodes family homes and disrupts neighbourhood balance. We want space for both students and residents: stop the spread in streets feeling the pressure, and create a place meant for students.

230357.explanation: Maastricht has no shortage of hotels, but a shortage of housing. Every prime location that is turned into a luxury or other hotel – like the old Cinema Palace – is an unacceptable missed opportunity to create real housing. So: stop with the hotels, and use the available space for affordable homes!

230358.explanation: Where possible, we want fewer parking spaces and more greenery in residential areas. Not just for appearances, but because trees and greenery provide cooling, drainage, and nicer play areas. Our approach is common-sense and neighbourhood-specific, without intending to rob people of their cars.

230359.explanation: Housing is a right, not a luxury. Every new development should include affordable homes up to €280,000, so that people on average incomes can continue to live here. No expensive developments for investors, but real homes for first-time buyers, young families, and residents who want to build a life in Maastricht.

230360.explanation: The SP believes that rental social housing is for everybody living, working and enjoying life in the city, not just for those who happened to be born within the municipal borders. Ties to the city can mean work, family, love, informal caregiving, or community life. Housing is a right, not a matter of background.

230361.explanation: Maastricht needs to expand its pool of affordable housing, which may mean expanding the city. But not at the cost of nature or landscape – on that the SP stands firm. We want expansion only where necessary and where it can occur responsibly, without impacting on nature, liveability, or health.

230362.explanation: Everyone has the right to live in a mobile home. Maastricht has let this lie for too long, and there is an unacceptable backlog for new sites. The SP wants a proactive, respectful approach: acknowledge the need, and speed up the provision of sufficient, suitable sites for our travelling communities.

230364.explanation: Problem debts keep people trapped in stress and poverty. Buying off debts under generous conditions gives people a genuine chance for a new start. Other municipalities have already achieved success with this approach. The SP wants to break the poverty cycle, so that people can move forward with their lives.

230365.explanation: The SP believes in participation, not coercion. Not everybody on welfare is able to perform community service, due to health, stress, or their domestic situation. In these cases, obligations have the opposite effect. Those who are both willing and able should have the opportunity, with support where necessary, and from a spirit of trust, not punishment.

230366.explanation: The SP wants to introduce a Maastricht city pass, similar to those already in use in other cities, to promote free or discounted participation for people on low incomes. Being able to go to the cinema or to watch Maastricht play football are part of a happy life, and nobody should be left out because they are on a budget.

230368.explanation: Rental social housing stock is already in short supply. Recent years have seen social housing sold off in swathes, with incredibly long waiting lists as a result. The SP wants more high-quality, geniunely affordable social rentals, offering everybody an affordable home – not fewer by selling them off.

230369.explanation: The SP wants a genuine low-traffic city centre. More parking spaces will have the opposite effect, making the city busier and less healthy. We instead want more space for greenery, pedestrians, and cyclists, with parking spaces reserved principally for those who have no choice but to travel by car.

230370.explanation: 30 km/h zones increase safety and liveability in residential areas. Slower speeds mean fewer accidents, less noise pollution, and more space for children to play outdoors and for seniors to get around safely. Neighbourhoods are for living in, not for tearing through at high speeds.

230371.explanation: Safe bicycle routes between neighbourhoods are not a luxury, but a basic need. For many people in Maastricht, the bicycle is their daily means of getting to work, school, and healthcare. Investing in safe, natural cycling routes will make cycling a safe and attractive option for all, young and old alike.

230372.explanation: The SP wants less on-street parking where possible, to make way for greenery, safe sidewalks, and pleasant streets. Not to bully car owners, but to make our neighbourhoods more liveable. Car parking will remain possible for those who need it, but the streets belong to everybody.

230373.explanation: Maastricht is at the centre of the Euregion. A train to Hasselt will make it easier for Maastricht locals to work, visit family, and go shopping in Belgium. Efficient, affordable public transport across the border is essential, especially for people without a car.

230374.explanation: Extending paid parking in the outer districts is necessary to keep neighbourhoods liveable. The streets are currently full of cars with German and Belgian licence plates. Residents and their guests should park for free, but people wanting to use our neighbourhoods as a parking lot need to pay, so the streets continue to belong to the neighbourhood.

230375.explanation: The SP wants a genuine low-traffic city centre. More parking spaces will have the opposite effect, making the city busier and less healthy. We instead want more space for greenery, pedestrians, and cyclists, with parking spaces reserved principally for those who have no choice but to travel by car.

230376.explanation: Young people are growing up in a world full of performance pressure, uncertainty, and stress. Too many of them are hitting a roadblock, and receiving help too late. The SP wants to invest more in preventive and other forms of youth mental health care: accessible and without any long waiting lists. None of them should face it alone.

230378.explanation: Informal carers keep our healthcare system afloat, often at the expense of their own time, health and incomes. The SP wants them to receive more financial support so they can keep going: with allowances, practical assistance, and recognition. Because caring for one another should not be a silent struggle.

230380.explanation: Menstruation poverty is becoming a real problem for more and more girls and women: pads and tampons cost money, and not everybody has that much to spare every month. Nobody should have to feel ashamed or resort to makeshift solutions. Tampons and pads should be free, just like toilet paper.

230320.explanation: The PVV believes that public administrators should be open and honest about who they meet with, and why. Residents should be aware of who is calling the shots in our municipality.

230321.explanation: Less use of consultancy firms. Municipal funds should be going to Maastricht citizens. Each year large amounts are channelled into costly agencies, while the results often end up in a drawer.

230322.explanation: Citizens deserve more opportunities to provide input.

230323.explanation: Administrators and public officials need to know what is really going on in society – but not at any additional expense.

230324.explanation: The city belongs to its residents, not to administrators, public officials, or action groups. Too often, far-reaching decisions are taken without consulting the citizens of Maastricht. A referendum ensures that the municipal administration has to listen to what the majority of residents genuinely want.

230325.explanation: We believe that no additional tax funding should go to large, costly projects such as Brightlands. Basic needs for residents should be in order first: affordable homes, safe neighbourhoods, good healthcare for seniors, and less bureaucracy and wastage.

230326.explanation: On the contrary, the OZB should be lowered. Maastricht residents are already facing rising costs for housing, energy, food, and healthcare. A higher OZB means even more costs for hardworking citizens, families, and seniors.

230327.explanation: Mass tourism is putting increased strain on the city. It is therefore only fair that visitors should help cover the costs. At the same time, a higher tourist tax must not impact local businesses, and the revenue should visibly benefit the city and its residents.

230328.explanation: These (Maastricht-based) businesses should not be burdened with additional municipal regulations. Compound legislation leads to higher costs and less freedom to do business.

230329.explanation: More and more municipalities are getting rid of the dog tax. As the capital, let us set the right example.

230330.explanation: First we should focus on the businesses in Maastricht itself.

230331.explanation: People’s schedules are becoming more and more hectic. Being able to do shopping on Sunday nights gives residents more flexibility in how to organise their time.

230332.explanation: What was intended as extra freedom in theory, has now become an obligation in practice. Let businesses decide for themselves whether to open on Sundays.

230333.explanation: Our city is FULL. Citizens of Maastricht have been on waiting lists for years, which is why Maastricht residents should receive priority, NOT recognised refugees.

230334.explanation: Primary responsibility lies with the university itself. It cannot be that municipal resources – so urgently needed for housing, safety, care, and quality of life – are used to solve problems caused by internationalization.

230335.explanation: Offering free language classes means Maastricht taxpayers will bear the cost for a responsibility that lies with the individual. It is reasonable to expect people who come to live in Maastricht to also make the effort to learn the Dutch language.

230336.explanation: Our city is full. We need to draw the line. Houses are scarce, services are under strain, the streets are becoming less and less safe. There is no more room.

230337.explanation: We support a fee reduction, so that residents are not burdened unnecessarily and the barrier to collection is lower.

230338.explanation: Community associations are the backbone of Maastricht. They depend mostly on volunteers, and are already overburdened with regulations and checks. Associations ought to be trusted, as long as they use alcohol responsibly and protect our youth. The municipality needs to support, not punish.

230339.explanation: The municipality should opt for safety and liveability, not subsidies or protections for cultural free spaces. Culture is a good thing, but no exceptions: the same laws should apply equally everywhere, and everybody should keep to the same rules.

230340.explanation: Dit verhoogt kosten en bemoeilijkt bouwen. Wij willen sneller en betaalbaar bouwen zonder extra regels zodat Maastrichtenaren sneller een woning krijgen.

230341.explanation: Wij zijn tegen het vergroten van spuitvrije zones bovenop landelijke regels. Extra lokale eisen maken landbouw en groenbeheer onnodig moeilijker en duurder. Landelijke normen zijn voldoende.

230342.explanation: PVV Maastricht is tegen nog meer stengere regels voor het kappen van bomen. Dit vertraagt projecten en maakt onderhoud onnodig ingewikkeld. De huidige regels bieden al voldoende bescherming.

230343.explanation: Maastricht is already a busy tourist city. Additional promotion is very costly, while residents are suffering more and more from crowding, housing shortages, and rising costs in the city centre. The municipality should invest more in services for residents.

230346.explanation: Het Geusseltbad is belangrijk voor zwemlessen, sport en gezondheid. Door te investeren in het Geusseltbad kan het beter voldoen aan de groeiende vraag van inwoners van Maastricht.

230347.explanation: The municipality already spends a lot on culture, additional investments are not necessary. The priority should lie with basic provisions for youth, such as meeting spaces and sport.

230348.explanation: Wij willen ons meer focussen op bestaande Maastrichtse feesten, lokale tradities en initiatieven van bestaande ondernemers in Maastricht.

230349.explanation: Water safety is essential. Not all children get the chance to learn to swim at home. By supporting school swimming, the municipality ensures that all children learn to swim.

230350.explanation: PVV Maastricht steunt extra investering in bijscholing en huiswerkbegeleiding. Jongeren verdienen gelijke kansen en goede ondersteuning. Dit versterkt onderwijs en toekomstperspectief voor gezinnen in Maastricht.

230353.explanation: Overlast, vervuiling onveiligheid nemen toe. Extra boa’s zorgen voor zichtbaarheid, snelle aanpak en een veiliger Maastricht voor inwoners.

230354.explanation: De gemeente moet inderdaad strenger optreden tegen fout geparkeerde fietsen. Ze zorgen voor veel overlast. Strakkere handhaving houdt Maastricht netjes en toegankelijke.

230355.explanation: Just the opposite: we need more video surveillance.

230356.explanation: Crowding and nuisance have risen dramatically in residential areas. Residents – seniors and families especially – have seen their safety and quality of life decrease due to the rise in student homes. Students are welcome, but not at the expense of our own residents, who should take precedence when it comes to safe and liveable neighbourhoods.

230357.explanation: De stad is al verzadigd en nieuwe hotels zorgen voor extra druk en overlast.

230358.explanation: PVV Maastricht is tegen het schrappen van parkeerplaatsen in woonwijken. Bewoners moeten hun auto dichtbij kwijt kunnen.

230359.explanation: Including affordable homes of up to €280,000 in every new development gives first-time buyers and families more opportunities, making the housing market more accessible.

230360.explanation: Maastricht residents who have been on waiting lists for years should always take precedence.

230361.explanation: Yes, but only where it is strictly necessary and can be done responsibly – Maastricht residents should always come first.

230362.explanation: We moeten meer meedenken met woonwagenbewoners. PVV Maastricht wil opkomen voor Maastrichtenaren die hier al lang wonen. Eerst onze eigen inwoners helpen.

230364.explanation: People with serious debts should be able to make a fresh start. Buying off the debt can help, but only under strict conditions, to ensure that help goes to those in Maastricht who genuinely need it.

230365.explanation: PVV Maastricht believes that welfare recipients should give something back to society where possible. It provides structure, contributes to the community, and keeps benefits fair for everybody who really needs them.

230366.explanation: PVV supports a pass for residents on low incomes, to allow concessions or free access to activities in society. This will support health and a sense of community in Maastricht, without high costs to residents.

230368.explanation: PVV Maastricht believes that social housing tenants should have the opportunity to purchase their home where possible, to increase home ownership and mobility. The municipality can encourage housing associations to offer this option for as long as it remains affordable for Maastricht residents.

230369.explanation: The city centre does not have enough (affordable!) parking, which limits accessibility for residents and visitors.

230370.explanation: Many streets are already safe enough, and extra zones will create even more frustration and unnecessary delays. We give preference to targeted enforcement in genuine problem streets.

230371.explanation: PVV supports more investment in safe bicycle routes between neighbourhoods. Good lighting, high visibility, and safe crossings will increase road safety for residents.

230372.explanation: Reducing parking capacity will increase parking problems in surrounding neighbourhoods. Accessibility should be top priority, which means adequate parking.

230373.explanation: We oppose this if Maastricht has to foot the bill. First invest in security, liveability, and accessibility within our own city.

230374.explanation: This is an unnecessary expense for residents, and solves very little. We would rather see affordable and accessible parking for residents.

230375.explanation: This will restrict accessibility for residents, seniors, and businesses.

230376.explanation: PVV Maastricht supports extra spending on mental healthcare for young people. Waiting lists are far too long, and problems compound easily. Timely and accessible care is essential to provide a healthy future for youth in Maastricht.

230378.explanation: Informal carers are indispensable in our society, and should receive more appreciation and support from the municipality. More resources could provide better respite care, less bureaucracy, and genuine appreciation for their efforts.

230380.explanation: PVV Maastricht believes that the municipality should not take on any additional duties or costs that are not part of essential services. Free menstruation products create ongoing costs and additional regulations; these resources could be better spend on existing, targeted schemes for people on low incomes.

230320.explanation: In light of recent experiences, we can see this is necessary. Transparency is an absolute necessity!

230321.explanation: The council and executive need to hold themselves accountable. We need to invest more internally, and if that is not possible, in local businesses. Care must be taken to ensure that outsourcing does not lead to a confirmation of what the commissioning body wants.

230322.explanation: More funding for residents to manage their own priorities is reasonable, however the current citizen-budget processes do not seem to be working optimally.

230323.explanation: No obligation, but where it fits within personal development plans, a civic placement is a good idea.

230324.explanation: The referendum must be binding in accordance with the Swiss model, and the options must be clearly defined.

230325.explanation: Decisions have been made regarding Brightlands’ development, and no additional resources are necessary.

230326.explanation: Any increase to the OZB will eventually be passed on to residents who are already having trouble making ends meet. The first response to financial challenges should be to control spending.

230327.explanation: Maastricht is an overheated tourist city. Financial disincentives can reduce growth, and ensure that it remains manageable.

230328.explanation: Authorities must be able to act in particular local situations, keeping in mind that Maastricht should remain competitive. That is the task of local administrators.

230329.explanation: 50PLUS believes it is right for the municipal costs of dog ownership to be financed by the dog tax.

230330.explanation: Spending should focus on improving facilities to make Maastricht an attractive place to live and work, instead of pouring more money into marketing.

230331.explanation: 50PLUS has always been against longer opening hours, as it will certainly impact community life and small business owners who have no choice but to follow suit.

230332.explanation: 50PLUS has always been against longer opening hours, as it will certainly impact community life and small business owners who have no choice but to follow suit.

230333.explanation: 50PLUS believes that this is discrimination against our own population. We need to solve the housing problem, but we will not do so by disadvantaging the local residents.

230334.explanation: Responsibility for integration lies with the university itself. In principle, courses should be offered in Dutch, which will lead to fewer students.

230335.explanation: There is no such thing as free lessons. Responsibility lies primarily with the asylum-seeker centres, education institutions, or other organisations.

230336.explanation: 50PLUS believes the statutory requirements are adequate. The asylum-seeker centre in Eijsden-Margraten was set up just outside Maastricht’s city limits, and is also impacting Maastricht residents.

230337.explanation: 50PLUS sees that incorrectly parked bicycles impact the safety of seniors and residents with limited mobility. Parking one’s bike correctly is basic decency. It is a major problem that needs to be addressed.

230338.explanation: Regulations should be enforced equally for both hospitality and clubs/associations – no stricter action is necessary.

230339.explanation: 50PLUS does not see the added value of these spaces.

230340.explanation: It will be cheaper, easier, and more effective to incorporate this immediately into all new developments. We want to preserve nature in the long term, and pass it on to future generations.

230341.explanation: 50PLUS considers it important that residents are not exposed to health risks.

230342.explanation: Nature must be protected. Sick or dangerous trees should be felled, but it should not be too easy to remove trees for development purposes.

230343.explanation: An economy that invests too much in tourism is vulnerable. Maastricht must guard against an economy based solely on tourism and the university, and work towards greater diversity.

230346.explanation: It would be better to build a swimming pool on the western side of town. That way, people can still go swimming somewhere in the city even during major maintenance work.

230347.explanation: There are more than enough activities for young people.

230348.explanation: Maastricht hosts enough major events. The MECC is responsible for organising and acquiring its own events.

230349.explanation: A fine idea, but experience has shown that school swimming is riddled with practical complications. We should, however, encourage parents to ensure that their children are safe in the water.

230350.explanation: This is primarily a duty for schools and parents. Where necessary, schools should provide options and resources for those who need extra support.

230353.explanation: 50PLUS sees that safety is still a concern in Maastricht. Enforcement remains a high priority.

230354.explanation: We need monitoring and effective measures by removing bicycles. These poorly-parked bicycles compromise the safety of seniors and residents with limited mobility.

230355.explanation: In this day and age, technology should be used to improve the city. The use of smart cameras, which can serve multiple purposes, should be recommended.

230356.explanation: Students generally live on different schedules to other residents, making further blending of these groups undesirable.

230357.explanation: There are more than enough hotels, and even more still under construction. We must guard against overkill.

230358.explanation: Parking spaces should be for residents, so they can park within a reasonable distance of their homes. If a neighbourhood has an excess of parking spaces, they should first of all be reclaimed for greenery.

230359.explanation: 50PLUS favours homes that can be lived in for a lifetime, promoting mobility and easing the housing shortage.

230360.explanation: 50PLUS understands very well that current residents want to keep living in the city, and wants to help make it possible.

230361.explanation: This is unnecessary, and also seems impracticable. There are still plenty of opportunities to expand upward. We need a proper assessment of which homes are needed in the future.

230362.explanation: This has been identified as a challenge, and also that mobile home sites are not easy to find in our city.

230364.explanation: 50PLUS wants to address the business model of collection agencies – which profit from other people’s debt – in order to give residents a fresh start, subject to clear safeguards against relapse.

230365.explanation: 50PLUS does believe that this depends on the individual. There are enough social initiatives that need help, however this should not displace actual employment.

230366.explanation: 50PLUS does not want residents to be left out of society for financial reasons. Care must be taken to ensure that people do not stop looking for work because they risk losing these benefits.

230368.explanation: However, care must be taken to prevent speculation and short-term resale of these homes.

230369.explanation: 50PLUS is in favour of a low-traffic inner city, with park-and-ride facilities and good connectivity on the edges of town.

230370.explanation: The time saved for drivers or other road users is minimal, but lowering the speed limit greatly increases neighbourhood safety. also making it easier and safer for children to play outdoors.

230371.explanation: Quality cycling routes help motivate people to travel by bike, an important element of the STOMP system. They encourage people to leave their cars behind and go by bike instead. Proper consideration should be given to the different users of cycling routes, including e-bikes.

230372.explanation: Residents should always be able to park their cars near their homes.

230373.explanation: The current train route is Maastricht-Visé-Luik-Tongeren-Hasselt. Ridiculous. Rather than getting bogged down in legal disputes, we should focus on better connecting Limburg’s two capitals and universities, including connectivity to Flanders. Keeping the railway bridge is essential in this regard.

230374.explanation: Maastricht residents do not want tourists parking in their streets. Unfortunately this is what happens, since Maastricht is not investing in P+R facilities, and family members and friends are the ones who pay the price.

230375.explanation: This will benefit the city, as well as pedestrians and cyclists. It may also be worth considering limiting the use of fatbikes on these streets.

230376.explanation: 50PLUS does not believe that more money is the solution. Necessary care should be provided within existing financial frameworks. However, it is also clear that care providers have not been handling their finances responsibly.

230378.explanation: The ageing population means that more informal carers are needed. The practical obstacles they encounter should be reduced as much as possible.

230380.explanation: 50PLUS wants residents to be well informed about where they can access these products. There are already enough locations, and educational institutions also have an important role to play.

230320.explanation: The Socially Active Citizens’ Party (SAB) supports transparency in administration, but not needless bureaucracy. A lobbying register could prove valuable, provided it is practical and genuinely provides information to residents.

230321.explanation: SAB believes that policy should be developed using in-house knowledge as much as possible. Consultancy firms are costly, encourage knowledge drain, and reduce control in the council. External expertise should only be used for specialist or temporary issues.

230322.explanation: The Socially Active Citizens’ Party (SAB) is in favour of citizen participation, but does not see the citizen budget as an end in itself. We must first establish whether the current budget effectively reaches all areas and produces results. More funding only makes sense if it can be shown to work.

230323.explanation: SAB believes that policy improves when administrators and public officials are aware of what is happening in practice. Regularly engaging with neighbourhoods and organisations increases understanding, improves decision-making, and aligns with SAB’s commitment to promote people-focused, practical governance.

230324.explanation: SAB recognises the importance of consultation, but does not see referendums as a standard instrument. For complex issues, SAB prefers effective participation, neighbourhood consultation, and clear decision-making by the council so that interests

230325.explanation: SAB recognises the importance of Brightlands Maastricht health Campus, but believes that additional municipal funding is only justified where employment, care, and residents will demonstrably benefit. Results and transparency first, then extra funding.

230326.explanation: SAB believes that the OZB has already significantly increased in recent years. In a time when many residents are under financial strain, further hikes are not a good idea. SAB prefers to focus on effective cost management and spending reviews, rather than raising taxes. In the event of a budget surplus, the BSGW should be lowered.

230327.explanation: SAB believes it is reasonable for visitors to contribute more to the costs of crowding, management, and services in Maastricht. This will take the burden off residents, and keep Maastricht accessible and liveable without any higher costs for the city.

230328.explanation: SAB believes that businesses need space to do business. Additional local laws on top of national regulations create unnecessary overheads and reduce security. The municipality should aim for clarity, predictability and practicality, not red tape.

230329.explanation: In SAB's view, the dog tax is unfair and outdated. Dog owners already pay for care and amenities, while the revenue does not even benefit dog policy. Acrapping the tax is fairer, and more in line with lower local taxes.

230330.explanation: SAB believes that good accessibility, affordable space and clear regulations are what make Maastricht attractive, not costly promotion campaigns. First work on creating the preconditions for business; promotion will follow in due course once the foundation is in order.

230331.explanation: SAB believes that Sundays and holidays should be available for rest and family. Mandating longer opening hours puts pressure on staff and small businesses. If businesses have freedom of choice, fine, but there should be no further obligation.

230332.explanation: SAB sees that opinions on Sunday trading are divided among residents and businesses. We therefore favour local discretion, tailored solutions, and citizen involvement. However, we are not inherently opposed to fewer Sunday trading days, if that reflects the wishes of the local community.

230333.explanation: SAB believes that rental social housing should be more fairly distributed, based on waiting time and urgency. Prioritising recognised refugees is not fair to other home seekers. Human-centred, temporary shelter remains essential, however.

230334.explanation: SAB believes international students should become a more integral part of the city. Strengthening cooperation with Maastricht University will improve integration, liveability and mutual understanding between students and residents.

230335.explanation: In SAB's view, language is the key to participation in society. Free language classes help international residents to integrate more quickly, find work, and establish social contacts in Maastricht, strengthening social cohesion and resilience.

230336.explanation: SAB believes Maastricht should meet its national obligations, but not more than that. Higher numbers will put strain on housing and facilities. SAB is, however, in favour of humane, temporary accommodation within clear and attainable limits.

230337.explanation: SAB believes €50 is extravagant and disproportionate. A lower rate of €15 is fairer, prevents bikes from being abandoned, and keeps enforcement proportional. The measure is intended to be correctional, not punitive.

230338.explanation: SAB believes that alcohol regulations should be enforced carefully and consistently, especially for the protection of young people. There is no need for extra-strict enforcement or additional monitoring; the focus should be on clear rules, prevention, and collaboration with clubs and societies.

230339.explanation: SAB has no explicit position on cultural free spaces, but supports local initiatives, grassroots culture, and community contact. Cultural free spaces are a part of this picture, provided they are safe, practical, and in balance with the city.

230340.explanation: SAB believes that new housing developments must be future-proof. Nature-inclusive elements, such as nesting boxes and greenery, strengthen biodiversity and quality of living, and have a place in practical and responsible construction provided they remain affordable and practicable.

230341.explanation: SAB believes that public health should be protected within national regulatory frameworks. Additional local prohibitions on top of national standards lead to uncertainty and a fragmented approach. The priority should be compliance with and enforcement of existing legislation, rather than the constant introduction of stricter local rules.

230342.explanation: SAB believes that trees need effective protection, but is hesitant to impose stricter and stricter rules. We should carefully enforce existing regulations, ensure replanting, and dutifully balance safety, liveability, and construction requirements in a practical manner.

230343.explanation: SAB believes that Maastricht does not need additional pressure from tourism. Putting less money towards promotion, and focusing on liveability, distribution, and quality will unburden residents and businesses, attracting visitors to the city without costly campaigns.

230346.explanation: SAB recognises the importance of the Geusseltbad pool, but believes that expansion is only justified if there is demonstrable need, costs are manageable, and there is clear added value for residents. We should first optimise use of existing facilities before turning to expansion.

230347.explanation: SAB believes culture is important for young people’s development and social contact. A bigger focus on affordable and accessible cultural activities strengthens talent, social cohesion, and opportunities. A targeted approach is necessary, close to young people and with visible results.

230348.explanation: SAB believes that events should not exceed the city’s capacity. We do not need a higher number, but the right events that maintain a balance between liveability, safety and costs. Quality and distribution take precedence over growth for growth’s sake.

230349.explanation: SAB believes that school swimming is important for safety and equal opportunities. Not all children learn to swim at home. Municipal support helps prevent the risk of drowning, and ensures that all children can take part fully and safely.

230350.explanation: SAB believes that training and homework assistance are essential for equal opportunities. Targeted support helps students and jobseekers move forward, prevents setbacks, and strengthens resilience. Investing in people produces lasting social benefits.

230353.explanation: The national government in The Hague is providing too little scope for more police presence on the streets, so SAB wants to allocate more funding to recruit, properly train, and equip municipal enforcement officers (BOAs) with the necessary powers.

230354.explanation: SAB believes that incorrect parking should be dealt with where it causes inconvenience or a lack of safety. Stricter enforcement must not become an end in itself. Enforcement should be combined with clearer parking locations and fair rules.

230355.explanation: SAB wants increased and faster deployment of surveillance cameras in high-disturbance areas, as well as additional mobile cameras, including in neighbourhoods.

230356.explanation: SAB believes that residential areas should first and foremost be liveable for residents. Unchecked growth of student homes leads to neighbourhood disruption and displacement. We need a balance: student housing should be concentrated in suitable locations, not in every residential neighbourhood.

230357.explanation: SAB believes that new hotels are only a good thing if they can be shown to contribute to the capacity and liveability of Maastricht. We don't want a blanket ban, but a discretionary approach based on quality, location, and added value for the city.

230358.explanation: SAB believes that greenery is important, but does not support the wholesale reduction of parking spaces in residential areas. Parking needs vary between neighbourhoods. A tailored approach is best: add greenery where possible, without unnecessarily impacting residents.

230359.explanation: SAP wants to encourage affordable homes, but is against fixed standards for every development. The housing market differs from place to place. Work with discretion, a mixture of housing types and affordability through efficient agreements, not rigid rules.

230360.explanation: SAB believes it is reasonable for current Maastricht residents on waiting lists should receive as much priority for social housing wherever possible. This will support fairness and community support, within national frameworks and with due regard for urgency.

230361.explanation: SAB believes that we need to build more houses, but that should not automatically mean expanding the city borders. We should first look at infill, re-use, and densification where possible. Expansion should only be considered where it is carefully planned, feasible, and environmentally sound.

230362.explanation: SAB believes that mobile home residents also have a right to suitable housing. Additional sites are necessary to eliminate shortages and safeguard equal treatment, according to clear agreements on location, liveability and management.

230364.explanation: SAB believes that buying off problem debts under strict conditions can help create opportunities for the future. It prevents long-term poverty and lowers costs for society, provided it is combined with guidance, accountability, and clear agreements.

230365.explanation: SAB is against mandatory mutual participation for benefits. Recipients not yet in a reintegration program could be encouraged to participate in voluntary, meaningful activities. Tailored plans, support, and creating opportunities are the priority – not compulsory requirements.

230366.explanation: SAB believes that social participation should not depend on income. A social pass makes sport, culture, and courses accessible to people on low incomes, supporting health, development, and social cohesion. The pass must not be stigmatised, however; a discount pass is therefore preferable, up to 100%.

230368.explanation: SAB believes that social housing tenants who are willing and able should have the opportunity to purchase their home, strengthening housing security and personal ownership. Social housing stock must be maintained, however, and sales carried out responsibly.

230369.explanation: SAB believes that parking in the centre of Maastricht must be balanced against liveability and accessibility. The goal should not be more or less, but the right amount for each area, with workable alternatives and clear choices.

230370.explanation: SAB prioritises road safety, and supports more 30 km/h zones in residential areas. Needs differ between neighbourhoods, so a discretionary approach is needed: 30km/h where it will genuinely improve safety.

230371.explanation: SAB believes that safe bicycle routes between neighbourhoods are essential for road safety, health, and accessibility. Targeted investments reduce accidents, encourage cycling, and make daily transport safer and more appealing for young and old.

230372.explanation: SAB does not want a wholesale reduction of on-street parking. Parking needs vary between neighbourhoods. A targeted approach is needed: improvements where possible, retention where necessary, and always with due regard for liveability and accessibility in Maastricht.

230373.explanation: SAB values accessibility, but has reservations when it comes to large-scale rail projects. Affordable and flexible options first deserve investigation, such as buses and high-quality public transport. A train should only be considered where the project is demonstrably feasible, costs are manageable, and there is added value.

230374.explanation: SAB believes paid parking in the suburbs will impact residents’ accessibility and quality of life. Parking problems vary between neighbourhoods and demand a tailored approach, not the blanket expansion of paid parking as a standard solution.

230375.explanation: SAB is not necessarily in favour of car-free streets. No blanket expansion, but consideration of each street with due regard for resident accessibility, businesses, and emergency services in Maastricht.

230376.explanation: SAB believes that the mental health of young people is crucial. Additional support for prevention, early intervention, and accessible services prevents more serious problems later and improves opportunities, well-being, and safety for young people in Maastricht.

230378.explanation: SAB sees informal care as indispensable. Additional support prevents burnout, keeps care affordable, and bolsters resilience. Supporting informal caregivers is human-centred, helps avoid escalated care, and reflects a social, practical approach.

230380.explanation: SAB believes that period products are basic needs. Free distribution combats poverty and exclusion, especially among young people and those on low incomes, and contributes to health, dignity, and equal opportunities.

230320.explanation: Openness is essential to good governance.

230321.explanation: The costs of external consultancy firms mount quickly. The municipality can spend its money more efficiently, and M:OED supports as much in-house expertise as possible.

230322.explanation: M:OED wants more direct democracy for resident involvement. A citizens’ budget does not contribute directly, since it is often only accessed by a small group, and does not benefit disadvantaged groups. Extra funding would be better spent on democratic innovations, such as a students’ council.

230323.explanation: Community contact is a good thing, but this is not a workable solution.

230324.explanation: Asking opinions is a good thing – referendums are not the answer.

230325.explanation: This funding could be used to help young people start a business, or for social/student housing in a vibrant area.

230326.explanation: Among other things, this will lead to higher rental living costs, which are already high enough.

230327.explanation: The municipality should invest in making the city more sustainable, and M:OED believes that tourists should contribute.

230328.explanation: Additional regulations give the municipality more flexibility to lead the way, in areas such as sustainability.

230329.explanation: The dog tax is a significant source of income for the municipality.

230330.explanation: A large variety of employment options is needed to keep young people in Maastricht.

230331.explanation: It is simply baffling that the supermarkets close at 6 p.m. Longer opening times can promote a more healthy and sustainable lifestyle, something that M:OED has always advocated for.

230332.explanation: Sunday trading days give working people the chance to go shopping.

230333.explanation: Everybody should still have a fair opportunity to find a home. But where need is acute, some asylum seekers should be eligible for priority.

230334.explanation: International and Dutch students are an important group that receives too little attention.

230335.explanation: We believe that everyone who wants to learn Dutch should have that opportunity, especially if it improves employment prospects.

230336.explanation: All cities need to do their part. Maastricht should not have to make extra effort.

230337.explanation: Maastricht is outrageously expensive compared to other cities, and now they want to make bicycle collection needlessly difficult, when bicycles are an essential means of transport. M:OED wants a bicycle-friendly Maastricht.

230338.explanation: Clubs and associations are important centres of social contact. We should support them, not make things more difficult for them.

230339.explanation: M:OED believes there should be more alternative cultural activities for young people. The Landbouwbelang is one good example.

230340.explanation: Nature-inclusive construction need not be more costly. It has positive health effects, and demonstrably increases the enjoyment of one’s home. Greening is the future, and also helps combat heat stress.

230341.explanation: There is clear evidence that pesticides are bad for public health.

230342.explanation: A green city is a pleasant city to live in.

230343.explanation: Maastricht is already on the tourist map, no additional funding is needed to attract more tourists.

230346.explanation: Because swimming is so good for health, there could certainly be more and varied outdoor swimming spots.

230347.explanation: M:OED believes there are too few cultural activities for the younger demographic. Young people should receive support to organise their own activities and events.

230348.explanation: Yes, M:OED believes these should be events for young people, such as festivals.

230349.explanation: Everyone should learn to swim, whatever their parents’ income. Swimming also contributes to the health of Maastricht residents.

230350.explanation: Extra support helps create equal opportunities, and should not be accessible only to wealthy residents.

230353.explanation: The only thing requiring more manpower is additional security in nightlife areas.

230354.explanation: The municipality should create more bike parking. M:OED does not want to punish cyclists because there is not enough.

230355.explanation: Provided the balance between privacy and safety is maintained, there is no need to reduce the amount of camera surveillance.

230356.explanation: There needs to be more student housing in the inner city. The municipality should not only build dull studio complexes. There is too much bureaucracy blocking solutions to the accommodation problem.

230357.explanation: The municipality should be very careful when allowing new hotels. However, hotels with innovative concepts should have the opportunity to come to Maastricht.

230358.explanation: A greener city is a pleasant place to live. Some parking spaces could be used for bicycle parking.

230359.explanation: It is important for young people to have access to affordable housing.

230360.explanation: Bringing the knowledge economy to the city means giving people outside Maastricht equal access to housing.

230361.explanation: Adequate affordable housing for young people is important.

230362.explanation: M:OED believes housing is important for all cultural groups, but prioritizes regular homes over additional mobile home sites. Our focus is on long-term housing and mixed communities to support integration and help students and first-time buyers find housing more easily.

230364.explanation: It is the only way for some people to escape their situation. It also prevents future problems, and allows people to quickly become fully-fledged members of society once more.

230365.explanation: We believe that everyone who is able should contribute to society in some way, through volunteer work for example.
