230366.explanation: M:OED believes this should be accessible to everyone – it is also important for students and others on low incomes. It is an opportunity to integrate students into Maastricht life.

230368.explanation: This will reduce the availability of social housing. Instead, housing developers should be building more for students and young people.

230369.explanation: Maastricht should work towards a low-traffic city centre, with fewer parking spaces and more room for cyclists, pedestrians and greenery.

230370.explanation: This will make for safer and more pleasant streets.

230371.explanation: We need a well-developed cycling network, both within and outside the city centre. something that M:OED has always advocated for.

230372.explanation: These spaces should be converted into greenery and bicycle parking. P+R locations are also needed at the edge of the city.

230373.explanation: It is important for Maastricht to have good train connectivity in order to function effectively as the gateway to Europe. A train to Hasselt will improve access to cities such as Brussels and Antwerp.

230374.explanation: In outer neighbourhoods, this often costs more than it brings in. P+R locations are also needed at the edge of the city.

230375.explanation: Completely agree, to make room for cyclists, pedestrians, and greenery. P+R locations are also needed at the edge of the city.

230376.explanation: Youth mental health care is facing massive problems, such as long waiting lists. We need a sustainable solution to address this problem.

230378.explanation: Informal carers do a lot for society, including reducing pressure on healthcare.

230380.explanation: Menstruation poverty is a serious problem. Nobody should have to stay home because of their period. Free access to these products is essential to create equal opportunities.

230321.explanation: Public officials should have the expertise to make judgements and take responsibility, instead of passing the buck and squandering millions on research agencies.

230322.explanation: Because citizen budgets never concern major issues, their impact is never great. It is more about giving groups the impression that they are exerting influence. They are only small neighbourhood matters, like a park bench, or a water fountain.

230323.explanation: Policy and implementation in the municipality are disconnected. Policymakers have too little notion of how policy works in practice, and the implementers have too little influence over policy. They should therefore not only do civic placements, but also discuss policy together. Otherwise interning serves little purpose.

230324.explanation: Give citizens a voice, in important matters especially.

230325.explanation: Citizens are likely unaware that millions have already been invested in Brightlands. Wherever there is money to spare, Brightlands usually comes out on top.

230326.explanation: No, the OZB is already very high in Maastricht.

230327.explanation: The last hike was fairly substantial.

230328.explanation: Regulations are sometimes needed to prevent undesirable outcomes – like delivery scooters blocking the sidewalks. Sometimes extra laws are needed.

230329.explanation: Most municipalities have already done away with the dog tax. Maastricht should follow suit.

230330.explanation: Instead of spending money on advertisements and promotion, the economics department needs to actively attract companies that provide well-paid, stable jobs suited to people who are currently unemployed. In other words: we need decisive action.

230331.explanation: Workers also need days off. There are enough times for shopping.

230332.explanation: Leave the decision to businesses themselves. If they earn nothing on a Sunday, they will close of their own accord.

230333.explanation: Mass immigration needs to stop, and all the Distribution Act does is enable it. We already have a shortage of housing, space, and natural surroundings for our own population – taking on more asylum seekers and recognised refugees is a bad idea. We are against preferential treatment of this group.

230334.explanation: The university is actively recruiting internationally, to the detriment of Dutch students. It should not only reap the benefits, but also accept the responsibilities that come with it, that are currently being shouldered by the population, the municipality, and the police. The council has been urging the university to do so for years, to no avail.

230336.explanation: We must not further enable mass immigration.

230337.explanation: Millions were spent on a bicycle garage at the station, yet bicycles are still left on sidewalks and town squares. It’s just selfish. That is why the €50 fee should remain, including for students. Sometimes people need a financial incentive to change their behaviour.

230338.explanation: When alcohol abuse leads to serious incidents, such as alcohol poisoning and vandalism at student clubs, for instance.

230339.explanation: Good to have an alternative available.

230340.explanation: We also need grassy areas and parks.

230341.explanation: Health takes precedence, and the pesticides also end up in the groundwater.

230343.explanation: Big crowds mean big money, is what the economic department thinks. This needs to change. Quality of life in the city is dropping, and mass tourism reduces the city’s appeal.

230346.explanation: There are waiting lists for swimming lessons, people with disabilities have no access due to overcrowding, more and more people are swimming for exercise.

230347.explanation: There should once again be a music school for young people, who can later progress to the conservatory. The lessons in painting, acting, etc. at Kumulus could potentially be made more affordable.

230348.explanation: The event calendar is chock-full, and we must not destroy liveability in the city altogether. There is plenty to do every weekend.

230349.explanation: Schools would then be required to organise the swimming lessons. So far, few schools have been willing to organise school swimming because it is too much work.

230350.explanation: This is a school responsibility, and the schools should organise it. The municipality has no authority here.

230353.explanation: The Liberal Party Maastricht (LPM) has been calling for this for years, but the executive refuses to cooperate. Evidently, enforcement is not a priority. Wages need increasing, but the answer is always no. Many enforcement officers have moved to other municipalities due to the low pay, and high workload due to sickness absence.

230354.explanation: The municipality knows where the bicycle problems are, but enforcement has no instructions to take action. Soft measures, such as charm offensives, do not work. Only direct cleanups help. The problem is solvable, but enforcement is being eroded and priority is low.

230355.explanation: More cameras are needed for greater security and to apprehend perpetrators.

230356.explanation: Student campuses need to be built, to free up family homes. Overmaze is one good potential location for a student campus.

230357.explanation: There are enough hotels – what we need is housing.

230358.explanation: Both are essential. People simply need cars.

230359.explanation: In 2020, the Liberal Party Maastricht successfully submitted the ‘more affordable housing’ motion, for Maastricht residents not on generous salaries, but on ordinary salaries. They, too, need to be able to afford housing here. We support the interests of the people, not investors’ bank accounts.

230360.explanation: Our own population should always take precedence over recognised refugees.

230361.explanation: A needs assessment is required, taking into account declining ageing trends and low population growth among local residents. Mass immigration must stop, it is putting enormous pressure on our country. There is a shortage of space, housing, and accommodation. Over 1000 new homes have already been built.

230364.explanation: This would cost millions. It would be better to ensure that people don’t get involved with shady companies or bad debt managers, and also offer coaching on how to handle money.

230365.explanation: If there is a labour shortage, we support paid employment over volunteer work. If that is not feasible, then volunteer work.

230366.explanation: What this pass aims to achieve is already available. For years, schemes have been available that provide for exactly these things.

230368.explanation: Housing stock needs to be kept up.

230369.explanation: Because there is a lot of new development going on in the city centre.

230370.explanation: People are driving far too fast.

230371.explanation: But we also need regulations governing fatbikes and electric bikes that go much too fast. They are usually a danger to pedestrians and other road users.

230372.explanation: If we want more homes, we will need more parking spaces.

230374.explanation: This should not be expanded unless the residents themselves ask for it.

230375.explanation: People also live in the inner city: can’t they drive home anymore? Will emergency services be banned from the city centre too? What about people with limited mobility, can they no longer be dropped off in those streets? How about pick-ups and deliveries for businesses? Will those be prohibited too?

230376.explanation: Schools should also focus more on young people who are not doing well, and combat bullying.

230320.explanation: A public lobbying register promotes openness and transparency, something that FVD Maastricht strongly supports.

230321.explanation: The city of Maastricht spends a fortune on external consultancy firms. This spending could be greatly reduced – public funds should be spent with the utmost care.

230322.explanation: FVD Maastricht would rather see direct input on important topics in the form of local referendums. Experience shows that the citizen budget often goes to people and organisations with good connections and a substantial network – far from ideal, in our view.

230323.explanation: By sending civil servants and officials into the community, we improve contact between the municipality and residents while also giving staff a much better understanding of what matters to people in the city.

230324.explanation: Democracy means more than just voting every four years. For important local issues – such as the placement of asylum-seeker centres, waste policy, or the introduction of car-free zones – Maastricht residents should be consulted.

230325.explanation: Right now, the municipality is pouring unbelievable amounts into Brightlands. It has already cost Maastricht taxpayers €150,000 to change the station name from Maastricht Randwyck to Maastricht Brightlands – symbolic of how money is being squandered in the area.

230326.explanation: The OZB is one of the most unfair forms of tax. It should be drastically lowered, and absolutely not raised.

230327.explanation: Keeping taxes low makes Maastricht an attractive place, which brings prosperity.

230328.explanation: Imposing more regulations than necessary is most unwise. Businesses are the backbone of Maastricht society and should be supported, not burdened with additional bureaucracy.

230329.explanation: Pet ownership should not be a cash cow for the municipality. FVD Maastricht strongly supports getting rid of the dog tax.

230330.explanation: These funds could be better spent on genuinely making Maastricht more attractive to businesses, instead of on an empty promotional campaign.

230331.explanation: Businesses should be free to decide on their own opening hours.

230332.explanation: Businesses should be free to decide on their own opening hours.

230333.explanation: It is absolutely ludicrous that people who have only been here a short time should take precedence over born-and-bred Maastricht residents, who have been on waiting lists for years.

230334.explanation: It is up to international students themselves to integrate into their new environment.

230335.explanation: Nothing is free. Essentially, ‘free language lessons’ means they are paid for by Maastricht taxpayers. There are currently enough accessible ways to learn a new language, such as DuoLingo.

230336.explanation: The municipality should not accept any more asylum seekers, for the simple reasons that we have a housing shortage, and it creates friction with the local population.

230337.explanation: Enough warnings are given before a bicycle even ends up anywhere near the depot.

230338.explanation: The municipality does not need to unfairly obstruct clubs and associations.

230339.explanation: The Landbouwbelang has been squatting in free accommodation for decades. In this respect, the authorities have already been extremely generous. If Landbouwbelang can find and pay for their own accommodation, fine, but it is not the municipality’s responsibility.

230340.explanation: First and foremost, new developments need to be realistic, and match the existing Maastricht architecture.

230341.explanation: Stricter policy than necessary is unwise.

230342.explanation: FVD Maastricht is against the unnecessary felling of trees. Current regulations are enough to ensure that trees cannot be so easily removed.

230343.explanation: Maastricht has an excellent reputation, and is already so popular that more promotion is not necessary. Promotion already occurs via word of mouth, when residents or tourists recommend Maastricht to others. Maastricht events also generate more good publicity.

230347.explanation: FVD Maastricht supports a greater focus on Maastricht culture among all age groups. Young people are no exception.

230348.explanation: Large events give the city extra allure, and also benefit the Maastricht economy.

230350.explanation: Training and homework assistance are the responsibility of parents along with the school.

230353.explanation: Although FVD Maastricht prefers police deployment, community enforcement officers can also help improve safety – as long as they are focused on protecting people, not generating revenue by collecting extra fines.

230354.explanation: FVD Maastricht is of the opinion that we should combat the growing problem of badly-parked bicycles.

230355.explanation: Not only is it unnecessary to have so many cameras everywhere, it is also an invasion of privacy.

230356.explanation: Halting all student housing growth is too harsh and infringes on property rights. Problems should be addressed where they occur, not by prohibiting student housing in advance.

230357.explanation: A blanket ban on new hotels in Maastricht is far too drastic a measure. FVD Maastricht favours a discretionary approach.

230358.explanation: Removing parking spaces is not a good idea, and will lead to practical problems. We must be realistic: cars are a necessity in the day-to-day lives of many.

230360.explanation: It is particularly sad that nowadays, more and more people cannot find a place to live in the area where they come from. The municipality should help them as much as possible.

230361.explanation: Maastricht is facing a housing shortage. Building more homes is one solution to the problem.

230362.explanation: The same principle applies to both regular and mobile homes: the municipality should ease legislation so the demand can be met.

230364.explanation: Buying off problem debts is a very bad idea. It signals that the municipality will ultimately always be there to bail people out. Furthermore, it is unfair for Maastricht taxpayers to bear the brunt of other people’s debts.

230365.explanation: People on benefits are perfectly able to contribute to society.

230366.explanation: FVD Maastricht is in favour of equal treatment for all residents.

230368.explanation: Although FVD Maastricht finds this idea appealing, the decision should ultimately rest with the housing association.

230369.explanation: FVD Maastricht is in favour of adequate parking throughout Maastricht, which also means the city centre.

230371.explanation: The current cycling routes in the Netherlands and Maastricht are already very high-quality.

230372.explanation: FVD Maastricht supports adequate parking throughout Maastricht.

230373.explanation: Effective connectivity to nearby cities is essential.

230374.explanation: The paid-parking zone in Maastricht is already enormous. Even in neighbourhoods far from the city centre, such as Scharn and Pottenburg, parking costs money. This is not a good idea.

230375.explanation: FVD Maastricht supports proper access to the city centre – for all modes of transport.

230378.explanation: Informal carers are essential to Maastricht society. Support can be given in many ways, such as parking permits.

230380.explanation: Nothing is free. In this case, Maastricht taxpayers will foot the bill. Besides, where do we draw the line? Why tampons and menstrual pads, but not toothpaste or toilet paper?

230320.explanation: The government should lay its cards on the table. If high-placed officials and members of the executive meet with lobbyists, then Maastricht should know who was at the table and what was discussed. No back-room politics, but honest government. That is the least residents deserve.

230321.explanation: Hiring a consultancy firm for every simple question? What a waste of money. Outside firms should only be used when strictly necessary: to obtain real specialist knowledge. The municipality should do its own thinking, not outsource everything to expensive external consultants.

230322.explanation: Residents have a right to provide input, but the municipality must take responsibility itself and not delegate everything to residents. We believe it is our duty to invest actively in neighbourhoods, without people needing to lobby first for every park bench or zebra crossing.

230323.explanation: Policymakers should know what is really going on. Spending a morning at the coal face is worth more than a hundred reports. We want administrators and officials with their feet firmly on the ground. No ivory towers, but genuine engagement with the city.

230324.explanation: Referendums can be valuable, but are not enough. Taking residents seriously means an ongoing process of listening, clarification, and changing course. The municipality needs dialogue with neighbourhoods – not simply asking a question, but genuinely working together on solutions.

230325.explanation: An investment in Brightlands is an investment in the future of Maastricht. It both draws talent and creates jobs at every level, from researchers to support staff, giving all residents more opportunities. Smart growth starts with prosperity that benefits everyone.

230326.explanation: Taxing homeowners more is not fair policy. MAASTRICHT van NU does not want to punish residents for owning their home. We want to focus on other solutions: higher tourist taxes, building vacancy, and more efficient use of public funds. More revenue without impacting residents.

230327.explanation: Tourists increase the demand for city services, so they should also contribute to the solution. MAASTRICHT van NU is in favour of a higher tourist tax, so that residents benefit: from clean streets, liveable neighbourhoods, affordable services, and additional efforts to combat poverty.

230328.explanation: Businesses are not ATMs. MAASTRICHT van NU is not in favour of compound policy, with local laws piled on top of national regulations. We want to make business easier, not harder. Less bureaucracy, more opportunities – that will keep Maastricht both liveable and economically robust.

230329.explanation: The dog tax is outdated and unfair, punishing dog owners while other pets can be kept for free. MAASTRICHT van NU wants both equal treatment and fewer nonsense fees. This token tax needs to go. It’s time for fairer policy!

230330.explanation: Maastricht sells itself just fine. MAASTRICHT van NU would rather invest in what genuinely attracts businesses: accessibility, affordable accommodations, and a healthy city. No costly campaigns, just investment in what works – for both businesses and residents.

230331.explanation: Supermarkets should be able to stay open longer, as long as it makes sense to do so. The additional hours are no problem, provided the business and employees are both in favour. MAASTRICHT van NU wants tailored solutions: freedom where it works, no pressure where it is not needed.

230332.explanation: Sunday trading has a place in a vibrant city. It is good for tourism, neighbourhood convenience, and employment. MAASTRICHT van NU wants modern flexibility, not nostalgia for a time when Sundays were for peace and quiet.

230333.explanation: The housing shortage affects all residents. MAASTRICHT van NU believes that everyone should have equal access to rental social housing. No automatic priority, but a fair assessment of the situation, family makeup, and urgency. Housing is a right, not a privilege.

230334.explanation: Integration starts with the students themselves and the university, not the municipality. MAASTRICHT van NU believes in addressing the housing and nuisance situation first, before investing extra time and money into more collaboration. Balance first, integration afterwards.

230335.explanation: Everyone in the city should be able to take part. Language is the key to work, social contact, and community engagement. MAASTRICHT van NU wants everybody to have the chance to learn Dutch – without barriers or cost. This is not an empty expense, but an investment in a cohesive society.

230336.explanation: Maastricht already has enough on its plate. MAASTRICHT van NU believes we should keep to the national agreements. Accepting more asylum seekers than required will place additional strain on housing, services, and community support. Let us take care of our own residents first.

230337.explanation: €15 is too low, €50 is too high. Low fees do nothing to solve the problem; high fees mean people will leave their bicycles in the depot. MAASTRICHT van NU prefers a fair middle ground: €25 is reasonable, encourages collection, and keeps the city in order. Common-sense enforcement.

230338.explanation: Clubs and associations deserve trust, not excessive supervision. MAASTRICHT van NU wants enforcement to focus on genuine problems, not everyday nannying. Encourage responsibility, and provide freedom to do so. Regulations are already there, but we need common-sense enforcement.

230339.explanation: Cultural free spaces have a place in a lively city. MAASTRICHT van NU supports room for creativity and grassroots initiatives – provided the rules are clear. No unchecked growth, no subsidised competition for businesses. Freedom, yes, but fair and responsible.

230340.explanation: Nature is important, but affordable housing is priority no. 1. Mandatory nesting boxes and green roofs drive up costs, while starters and families have trouble even finding a home. Encourage it where possible, but it should not be mandatory. You can't afford a nesting box without a roof over your head.

230341.explanation: MAASTRICHT van NU believes that the current national regulations are adequate. Extra prohibitions will cause confusion and put unnecessary pressure on farmers and green space managers. Health is important, but policy must remain implementable and fair. National direction, local stability.

230342.explanation: Trees are valuable, but sometimes we need to make room for safety, housing, or to improve liveability. MAASTRICHT van NU wants to ease bureaucracy, while maintaining due care. Case-by-case evaluation with due regard for nature and common sense.

230343.explanation: Maastricht sells itself just fine. MAASTRICHT van NU wants less money spent on glossy campaigns, and more on liveable neighbourhoods and affordable homes. Tourism is welcome, but the city's own interests take precedence. Let’s first invest in who lives here, before thinking about the passers-by.

230346.explanation: The Geusseltbad is more than just a swimming pool. It is a place for sports, swimming lessons, and cooling off – especially for those who cannot go away for the holidays. It is too busy in the summer months. MAASTRICHT van NU wants to investigate the best and most affordable way to expand. The Geusseltbad belongs to all of us – let us invest in it together.

230347.explanation: Culture is not an afterthought – it gives young people a voice, an outlet, and opportunities. MAASTRICHT van NU wants to invest more in culture that genuinely appeals to the younger generation: music, dance, street art, film. Less funding for elitist concert halls, and more for young talent on the streets.

230348.explanation: MAASTRICHT van NU is satisfied with the current policy. We should not be pushing for even more major events at any cost. Quality over quantity, with due regard for liveability and community support. The city is for the residents, not just the day-trippers.

230349.explanation: All children should learn to swim, for both safety and health. Not all parents can afford swimming lessons. MAASTRICHT van NU therefore wants to bring back school swimming lessons, with support from the municipality. Swimming has to be learned. It is not a luxury, but a fundamental right.

230350.explanation: Not all children receive help with school at home, and tutoring is expensive. MAASTRICHT van NU supports free neighbourhood homework assistance and training for people seeking better employment. That will help children and parents alike. A little help can make a big difference.

230353.explanation: Visible enforcement creates safe neighbourhoods and reduces incidents. MAASTRICHT van NU wants BOAs on the streets, not behind desks. In the neighbourhood, approachable and with authority. For liveability, safety, and trust in public spaces.

230354.explanation: Incorrectly parked bicycles create chaos and disrupt pedestrians, especially the elderly and people with decreased mobility. MAASTRICHT van NU want more visible, fair, and targeted enforcement. Not a bicycle dump on the pavement. The city belongs to us all.

230355.explanation: Cameras are not a magic solution, but they do help with security and investigations. MAASTRICHT van NU does not want big brother, but surveillance where it is needed: at high-risk locations, dumping sites, and in nightlife areas. Fewer cameras does not make for a better city.

230356.explanation: Homes are for living in, not slicing up for a profit. MAASTRICHT van NU wants to re-establish the balance: no unchecked growth of student housing to the detriment of families and community. Students are welcome, but distribution and clear boundaries are needed for liveable neighbourhoods.

230357.explanation: Maastricht has enough hotels. In our view, we do not need any more. MAASTRICHT van NU wants to preserve the balance between tourism and liveability. While more hotels does not automatically equate to more value for the city, they do create more crowding. Setting limits is good governance.

230358.explanation: Green spaces are important, but so is accessibility. MAASTRICHT van NU does not want to remove parking spaces if residents will suffer. We must first look at alternatives, tailored to each area. More greenery? Yes please! But let's not chase cars away without a clear plan.

230359.explanation: Not all development plans are the same. MAASTRICHT van NU believes in examining the needs of each location. Sometimes affordable owner-occupied homes make more sense, other times it's social housing, or even care residences. A blanket standard will not work – tailored solutions do. We should build what we really need.

230360.explanation: People who grow up in the region should be able to continue living here. MAASTRICHT van NU wants to prioritise our own home seekers, to keep neighbourhoods lively, promote clubs and associations, and keep families close together.

230361.explanation: MAASTRICHT van NU wants space for new homes, including at the fringes of the city if necessary. The housing shortage is too serious for inaction. City expansion should be an option, provided it is well-considered, affordable, and with due regard for nature and liveability. We should build for the future, not for show.

230362.explanation: Mobile home residents are a part of the city, and MAASTRICHT van NU wants them to have equal access to living space. The only way to do so is with more sites. No discrimination, no delays – just do what is necessary for equal treatment and genuine freedom of choice.

230364.explanation: MAASTRICHT van NU wants long-term solutions, not temporary fixes. It should be possible to buy out problem debts under strict conditions, with a solid plan, mutual commitment, and clear agreements. Not a handout, but a second chance that really works.

230365.explanation: People who are able to work should give something back to the community. MAASTRICHT van NU believes that welfare should not be a final destination. Useful work provides rhythm, pride, and future prospects. Participation according to ability, with proper support and understanding for a person’s capabilities.

230366.explanation: Participation in society should not be an optional extra. MAASTRICHT van NU wants a social pass for people on low incomes, so that they too can enjoy sports, education, and culture. Equal opportunities start with access, not a price tag. This is investing in inclusion.

230368.explanation: Those who are willing and able should have the option to purchase their rental home. MAASTRICHT van NU wants housing associations to actively make this possible. It strengthens ownership, mobility, and neighbourhood connection, as well as additional revenue for the associations to re-invest in more social housing.

230369.explanation: More parking spaces in the city centre solve nothing, but instead create additional crowding, pollution, and reduce space for pedestrians and cyclists. MAASTRICHT van NU wants smart mobility: good access, but with due regard for liveability and space in the city.

230370.explanation: Road safety begins with speed. MAASTRICHT van NU wants to introduce 30km/h on more residential neighbourhood roads, especially in places with lots of children, seniors, and cyclists. Liveability and safety take precedence over speed.

230371.explanation: Cycling should be safe and straightforward – even between neighbourhoods. MAASTRICHT van NU wants to invest in clear, well-lit cycling routes. No winding pathways or unsafe crossings, but real routes that allow young and old to pass through safely.

230372.explanation: Fewer parking spaces sounds good for nature, but often creates more frustration than improvement. MAASTRICHT van NU first wants to look at alternatives, such as parking garages or shared transport. No political point-scoring, but plans that genuinely work for the local area.

230373.explanation: A train to Hasselt would strengthen the region, connect people, and offer sustainable transport. MAASTRICHT van NU sees it as an intelligent investment in accessibility, the economy, and cross-border collaboration. More connection, less congestion – that's progress.

230374.explanation: Paid parking should not be extended in outer districts. It is the tourists – not the residents – who cause crowding. MAASTRICHT van NU wants higher rates for visitors, not for residents. More resident support, such as 500 hours of free parking time per year. Fair parking, without additional costs.

230375.explanation: Low-traffic zones can work, but we do not need to shut down everything completely. MAASTRICHT van NU wants tailored solutions: space for pedestrians where possible, access where needed. No blanket war on cars, but smart choices, street-by-street – with residents and businesses at the table.

230376.explanation: More and more young people are grappling with mental health problems. MAASTRICHT van NU wants investment in fast, accessible help. No waiting lists, but genuine support at school, in the neighbourhood, and online. Mental health while growing up is just as important as education or sport.

230378.explanation: Caregivers are indispensable, but often overworked. MAASTRICHT van NU wants to provide more support: respite care, acknowledgement, and practical help. Not only when things go wrong, but proactively and close to home. Those who care for others also deserve care and attention from the municipality.

230380.explanation: MAASTRICHT van NU acknowledges the problem of period poverty, but wants targeted support where it is really needed. Not handing out free products everywhere, but ensuring that those who need them have easy access through schools, support services, or community centres. Practical and goal-oriented.

statements.title: The municipality should abolish the dog tax.

titleParts.glossary: A tax that owners of one or more dogs must pay to the municipality.

statements.moreInfo: In Maastricht, dog owners must pay a dog tax. The municipality can use the revenue from this tax to pay for dog facilities, such as dog parks and cleaning up dog droppings. It can also use the funds for other purposes, however. In 2026, the Maastricht dog tax is set at €77.52 for the first dog.
statements.pro: The dog tax is unfair. There is no tax for other pets.Lots is spent on administration and enforcement.The dog tax is outdated. Many municipalities no longer have a dog tax.
statements.con: The dog tax generates revenue for the municipality.The money can be used to pay for dog-related expenses.The ‘polluter pays’ principle should apply.

statements.title: The fee for collecting a bicycle from the impound should be lowered to €15. The current fee is €50.
statements.moreInfo: The municipality of Maastricht removes bicycles that are broken, have not been used for a long time, or are parked incorrectly, in order to free up space in bike racks and parking garages. In the centre of town, bicycles are also removed that are on the sidewalk or not in designated parking areas. Removed bicycles are taken to the bicycle depot at Gerardusweg 39, where owners can collect them. To do so, they currently pay a fee of €50.
statements.pro: €50 is too much to collect your bike.If the amount were lower, people would collect their bikes sooner.If the city wants to encourage cycling, they should lower the fee.
statements.con: Higher fees encourage people to follow the rules.Lower prices can lead to more problems due to incorrectly parked bicycles.The €50 fee covers municipal expenses.

statements.title: The municipality should offer free language courses to international residents.
statements.moreInfo: Maastricht has many international residents, such as students, expats, and people with residence permits. The municipality could decide to offer free language classes to international residents. Language classes are already compulsory for some groups. 2 The law already requires people with residence permits to take language classes. While not compulsory for asylum seekers in asylum-seeker centres, classes are still offered there. Students can take language classes through their school or university. They decide for themselves whether to do so. Expats can take part voluntarily in language courses or initiatives in the city.
statements.pro: Language helps international residents to participate in society. Language classes allow people to find work sooner. Learning a language promotes social contact.
statements.con: Language classes should be organised nationally, not by the municipality. Lessons do not need to be free for everybody. People who can afford it can take a course themselves.

statements.title: Maastricht should introduce a public lobbying register, creating a record of who meets with administrators and why.
statements.moreInfo: Municipal office-holders, such as councillors and the Mayor, regularly meet with organisations, businesses and interest groups. This is also referred to as lobbying. A public lobby register records who has spoken with office-holders and what the meeting was about. Residents can consult this register.
statements.pro: A lobbying register provides more transparency in government.Residents can see who is trying to exert influence.Transparency increases trust in the municipality.
statements.con: It can be a barrier for people and organisations to contact the municipality.Maintaining a lobbying register takes a lot of time and money.A public lobbying register goes too far.

statements.title: The municipality should invest more in a campaign to promote Maastricht as an attractive business location.

titleParts.text: The municipality should invest more in a campaign to promote Maastricht as an attractive

titleParts.glossary: A location where a business establishes itself.

statements.moreInfo: Municipalities can attract businesses by showing what they have to offer. One way to do this is via a promotional campaign, which aims to persuade businesses to come to the municipality.
statements.pro: A promotional campaign will help bring businesses and talent to Maastricht. A promotional campaign could help generate more employment. More companies will contribute to the city’s economic growth.
statements.con: Maastricht is already very attractive to companies. This money could be better spent on accessibility and affordable business space. Creating a good business climate is more important than advertising.

statements.title: The municipality should try to attract more major events.

titleParts.text: The municipality should try to attract more major

titleParts.glossary: Temporary gatherings of an organised nature, such as street parties, flea markets, or music festivals.

statements.moreInfo: Each year, Maastricht hosts many large events, such as trade fairs, concerts, and sports and cultural events. These events often attract large numbers of visitors, both from within and outside the city. The municipality plays an active role, in cooperation with external partners, in updating and strengthening the programme of larger events.
statements.pro: Major events attract many visitors to the city.Events generate more income for businesses.Events make the city more attractive and vibrant.
statements.con: Large events can be disruptive to residents.They impact liveability, with crowding and noise.The city should also consider the quality of events and the level of community support, not just their number.

statements.title: Maastricht needs less video surveillance (security cameras).

titleParts.glossary: Monitoring of a building, area, or groups of people using security cameras (CCTV).

statements.moreInfo: The municipality uses security cameras to monitor disturbances and crime. They are installed in public spaces, and take footage of what happens there. This footage is monitored by a control room to increase safety. Most cameras in Maastricht are located in the city centre, Wyck, the nightlife area, and around De Geusselt football stadium. The municipality is expanding video surveillance to include the entire city centre and neighbouring areas. In total, there will be around 110 permanent security cameras. There are also cameras on the access roads to the inner city, to monitor for any cars that should not be there because they emit too many harmful emissions. The municipality can also add temporary cameras at hotspots or when there are major events.
statements.pro: Security cameras are an invasion of privacy. Cameras do not solve the problem, they only move it somewhere else. It is not necessary to put cameras everywhere.
statements.con: Cameras create a greater feeling of safety. Cameras are necessary at hotspots for crime or disturbance. Video surveillance helps investigate incidents.

statements.title: Recognised refugees should not receive priority for rental social housing.

titleParts.text: Recognised refugees should not receive priority for

titleParts.glossary: Rental homes with an initial rent that does not exceed a fixed amount. In 2026, this amount is €932.93 per month.

statements.moreInfo: Recognised refugees are people who have applied for asylum and received a residence permit. They are allowed to stay in the Netherlands. Every six months, the national government determines how many recognised refugees each municipality must accommodate. These numbers are based on the population in each municipality. Municipalities can decide for themselves who receives priority for rental social housing. Recognised refugees are not automatically prioritised – that depends on municipal regulations. In Maastricht, roughly 1,100 social rentals become available each year. Around 5-6% of these are prioritised for recognised refugees.
statements.pro: Everybody deserves the same opportunity for rental social housing.Giving priority to refugees means starters and young people need to wait longer for housing.Recognised refugees can live in temporary modular housing, and do not need priority social housing.
statements.con: A permanent home helps refugees participate in society.The municipality is required by law to provide recognised refugees with housing.The housing shortage was not caused by refugees, but by housing policy in recent years.

statements.title: Maastricht should invest in a train line to Hasselt in Belgium.
statements.moreInfo: Maastricht and Hasselt are close to each other, but there is no train that travels between them. The only current options are a bus, or a lengthy train route. A direct line could improve public transport across the border. The municipality could work with other authorities to create one.In the past, there were plans for a light rail between Maastricht and Hasselt. The project was cancelled, and there are still legal proceedings underway between authorities in the Netherlands and Belgium.
statements.pro: A train line would strengthen cooperation in the region.A train to Hasselt would make it easier to travel on to cities such as Brussels and Antwerp.Travelling by train is sustainable, and good for the public transport system.
statements.con: There is already a good bus connection between Maastricht and Hasselt.Train lines are extremely expensive.The Belgian partners cancelled the light rail project – without certainty on the costs, starting a new project would be unwise.

statements.title: The municipality should allow supermarkets to remain open until 10:00 p.m. on Sundays.
statements.moreInfo: The municipality of Maastricht sets regulations for store opening hours. They act as a framework: retailers are not required to be open for late-night shopping or Sunday trading. Supermarkets are subject to different times than other retailers. On Sundays, Maastricht supermarkets are currently allowed to be open from 8 a.m. until 6 p.m.
statements.pro: Residents will have more freedom to decide when to do their shopping.Grocery shopping will become easier for people who work full-time.Businesspeople should be able to decide for themselves when to open their supermarkets.
statements.con: Sundays should be for peace and quiet, family, and free time.Many small businesses will not be able to keep up with these extended hours.Longer opening hours put more pressure on employees.

statements.title: All new builds should be nature-inclusive. This means that homes should contribute to natural development, through additions such as inbuilt nesting boxes.

titleParts.glossary: Takes plants and animals into greater consideration.

titleParts.text: . This means that homes should contribute to natural development, through additions such as inbuilt nesting boxes.

statements.moreInfo: Municipalities can set criteria for new housing developments. Nature-inclusive construction takes nature and biodiversity into consideration, with additions such as nesting boxes for birds and bats, green roofs, or space for plants and insects.Nature-inclusive building is currently not mandatory in Maastricht, however the municipality does issue recommendations on nature-inclusive elements. Future plans state that Maastricht wishes to work toward a standard for new developments that takes nature into consideration.
statements.pro: Nature-inclusive construction is good for the plants and animals in the city.Green neighbourhoods make for a healthier living environment.Neighbourhoods will be more heat-proof and flood-resistant.
statements.con: Nature-inclusive construction can raise the cost of homes.Additional regulations can delay housing developments.The main concern is for new builds to match the existing buildings in Maastricht.

statements.title: The inner city of Maastricht needs more parking spaces.
statements.moreInfo: The municipality of Maastricht sets parking policy in the city. Space in the city centre is limited, and is shared by cars, cyclists, pedestrians, trees, and outdoor eating and drinking areas.
statements.pro: More spaces will keep the inner city accessible to both visitors and businesses.People dependent on their car for travel will have more parking options.New construction projects demand more parking spaces.
statements.con: More parking spaces will lead to more traffic and congestion in the city.The centre should have more space for pedestrians and cyclists, and less for cars.The edge of the city is a better place for parking spaces.

statements.title: The municipality should invest more in cultural activities for young people.
statements.moreInfo: The municipality can decide to fund cultural activities for young people, such as music, dance, film, and other creative projects. These activities can be organised by the young people themselves, or by cultural organisations.In recent years, Maastricht has had special schemes for young people, such as subsidies for youth culture and school music projects. Municipally-funded cultural institutions also run activities for young people.
statements.pro: Culture helps young people to develop their talents.Cultural activities give young people opportunities to meet one another.The range of cultural activities for young people is currently too limited.
statements.con: The municipality already spends a lot on culture, they do not need to spend more.There are already enough cultural activities for young people.The money could be better spent on sports and other places where young people meet.

statements.title: The municipality should expand the city borders to increase housing construction.
statements.moreInfo: The municipality can build new homes on sites within the city, such as empty lots or old business premises. Building can also take place outside the city – this is called ‘expansion.’The plans in Maastricht currently prioritise building new homes within city limits. One option is to do so in larger urban development areas or existing neighbourhoods, close to services. If building inside the city is not enough, the municipality can consider extending the city limits. The municipality can decide where and how to do so.
statements.pro: This will create more housing. It could help address the housing shortage. It will allow affordable homes for young people to be built.
statements.con: There is enough room to build inside the city. Building outside the city will encroach on nature areas. City expansion could lead to building vacancy later.

statements.title: A speed limit of 30 km/h should apply to more locations in residential areas.

titleParts.glossary: The highest speed that cars are allowed to drive on the road.

titleParts.text: of 30 km/h should apply to more locations in residential areas.

statements.moreInfo: The ‘built-up area’ is the area within a city or town where people live, work, and shop. Currently, many of these areas have a maximum speed limit of 50 km/h, though some have already been reduced to 30 km/h.
statements.pro: 30 km/u is safer for residential areas. Slower traffic is better for children and seniors. 30 km/u makes streets calmer and more pleasant to live in.
statements.con: More 30km zones will lead to more delays and frustration. Through-roads were made for 50 km/u, and are often already safe enough. It is better to focus on streets where there are real problems.

statements.title: The municipality should invest more in Brightlands Maastricht Health Campus.
statements.moreInfo: Brightlands Maastricht Health Campus is a place in Randwyck where research is conducted on health, care, and innovation. It is home to companies, researchers, and education institutions.
statements.pro: More funding for Brightlands will create more jobs in Maastricht. The campus brings talent and businesses to the city. Investments can generate economic growth for the entire city.
statements.con: The municipality has already spent a lot on Brightlands. Extra funding only makes sense if there is a clear benefit. Municipal funding could be better spent on other city plans.

statements.title: The municipality should accept more asylum seekers than is nationally required.

titleParts.glossary: People who have applied for asylum and are still waiting for a decision on their case.

statements.moreInfo: People fleeing their country can apply for asylum. The Immigration and Naturalisation Service (IND) decides whether they can stay in the Netherlands. During this procedure, asylum seekers need accommodation.Under national agreements, Maastricht must host at least 493 asylum seekers. The municipality currently accommodates more than the required number. In total there are over 800 available places, including a permanent asylum-seeker centre (AZC) and a temporary emergency shelter.
statements.pro: A large city can absorb more asylum seekers than a small village. Accommodating more eases the shortage of places elsewhere in the Netherlands. We should offer a safe haven for people fleeing war.
statements.con: Maastricht should keep to the agreed national numbers. More refugees puts additional strain on the housing market. The city already has a large asylum-seeker centre, and already takes in a lot of people.

statements.title: There should be stricter enforcement on incorrectly parked bicycles.
statements.moreInfo: Bicycles are sometimes parked where they do not belong, such as on sidewalks or at building entrances, creating blockages or unsafe situations. The municipality can monitor for this, removing bicycles or issuing fines. The municipality removes bicycles that are broken, or that have not been used in a long time. In the Netherlands, a broken bicycle is called a ‘bicycle wreck.’ A bicycle that has been abandoned for some time is called an ‘orphaned bicycle.’ Bicycles parked incorrectly (outside designated areas or on sidewalks) can also be removed.Additional monitoring takes place in the city centre and around the station. Removed bicycles are stored at the bicycle depot on Gerardusweg. Bicycle wrecks are not stored. The municipality decides on how strictly to monitor bicycle parking.
statements.pro: Incorrectly parked bicycles cause problems for pedestrians. Seniors and people with disabilities are especially impacted. Stronger enforcement is essential to a safe and accessible cycling city.
statements.con: There is not enough bicycle parking in the city. Stricter enforcement only punishes cyclists for a lack of parking. Awareness campaigns and better parking facilities work better than fines.

statements.title: The municipal property tax (OZB) should be raised.

titleParts.glossary: A tax paid by the owners of buildings or land, or by the users of business premises.

statements.moreInfo: The municipal property tax (OZB) is a tax that people pay if they own a home or other property. The amount payable depends on the value of the home or building, and the percentages set by the municipality. The municipality uses OZB revenue for things like roads, green spaces, and resident services.
statements.pro: People who own an expensive home can afford to pay more tax than those with less-valuable homes. The OZB in Maastricht is lower than in comparable municipalities, and could therefore be raised a little. A higher OZB could generate revenue to build new, affordable housing.
statements.con: Increasing the OZB will raise living costs for residents. The OZB has already been raised in recent years.The municipality should look to reduce spending first.

statements.title: The municipality should ask Maastricht residents their opinion on important matters via a referendum.

titleParts.text: The municipality should ask Maastricht residents their opinion on important matters via a

titleParts.glossary: A public vote in which residents can give their opinion on a political issue. The municipal council is not legally bound to implement the outcome.

statements.moreInfo: The municipality of Maastricht has introduced a referendum by-law, allowing the municipal council to decide to hold a referendum. A referendum enables residents to give their opinion on an important topic. A referendum can only be held on a certain subject if a majority of the council votes in favour of it. In the Netherlands, the results of referendums are not ‘binding,’ which means that the council can ignore the result and do something else.
statements.pro: A referendum is a good way to find out what citizens think. On genuinely serious issues, residents themselves should have the final say. Referendums ensure that the municipal administration listens to residents more closely.
statements.con: Referendums only allow a yes/no answer, which leaves little room for nuance. For complex issues, consultation and citizen participation are more effective than a referendum. Referendums cost a lot of time and money, with little benefit in return.

statements.title: Maastricht should prioritise local home seekers in the allocation of rental social housing wherever possible.

titleParts.text: Maastricht should prioritise local home seekers in the allocation of

titleParts.glossary: A leased home with a low rent for people on low incomes (in 2026, the upper limit was set at €932.93 per month).

statements.moreInfo: Municipalities can decide on their own rules for allocating social housing. These rules are set out in a ‘housing ordinance,’ which can be used to determine what kind of home seekers get priority. Some groups can be allocated priority, such as informal carers or people living in temporary shelters. Since 2017, people with a residency permit do not automatically qualify for priority. Municipalities can decide for themselves whether they do.Municipalities can also specify other groups, such as those with strong ties to the local area. Currently in Maastricht, at least 45 per cent of the rental social housing that becomes available is allocated to home seekers from Maastricht.
statements.pro: Residents who have been on waiting lists for a long time should receive priority. People who grew up in Maastricht should be able to continue living here. Local priority fosters strong neighbourhoods and social networks.
statements.con: People from outside Maastricht also deserve a fair opportunity to find housing. Housing shortages are solved by building more housing, not by changing the housing ordinance. Rental social housing is for everybody who lives in and is a part of the city, not just people who grew up here.

statements.title: The municipality should introduce a ‘social pass,’ granting people on low incomes free or discounted access to sports, culture, or education.
statements.moreInfo: The municipality can help people on low incomes to participate in sports, cultural activities, or community classes. A social pass is one way to do so, by giving people free or discounted access to activities such as sports organisations, the theatre, or community education. Maastricht already has schemes that foster low-cost resident participation, such as the Youth Sports and Culture Fund and the Adult Sports and Culture Fund. The municipality could decide to supplement these schemes via a social pass.
statements.pro: A social pass will allow people on low incomes to take part in sports, cultural activities, and community courses. Participation in society should not depend on income. Everyone should have the same opportunities. A social pass lowers barriers, and makes it easier to take part in society.
statements.con: Schemes already exist that allow people on low incomes to participate in society. Yet another special scheme will just make the system more confusing. This has already been tried in the past, and it did not work well.

statements.title: Each new housing development should include homes for sale up to €280,000.
statements.moreInfo: When building new housing, the municipality makes agreements with project developers on what kinds of homes should be built. These can include rentals, owner-occupied housing, or a mixture of both. The municipality can set requirements for the price of homes for sale; one way to do so is to set a maximum price. A purchase price of up to €280,000 means homes that are relatively affordable.In Maastricht, the requirements say that when building 10 or more homes, at least 60% must be affordable, i.e. social rentals with a maximum monthly rent of € 932.93, or owner-occupied homes up to €280,000.
statements.pro: Affordable owner-occupied homes will mean that more people can buy a house.  First-time home buyers and young families will have more opportunities in the housing market. Housing needs to remain affordable for people on an average income.
statements.con: Fixed requirements in every development plan can lead to fewer homes being built. Not every location is suitable for all types of housing; they need to be tailored to the site. Rigid regulations can serve to slow mobility on the housing market.

statements.title: The municipality should enforce alcohol regulations at clubs and associations more strictly.
statements.moreInfo: Serving alcohol is subject to strict regulations, which are set out in both national and municipal laws. These laws also apply to clubs and associations, such as sports clubs, which therefore cannot serve alcohol to people aged under 18. The municipality monitors whether clubs follow the regulations.In Maastricht, it is municipal enforcement officers (BOAs) that do this. They perform checks, especially on busy days and during events, when they pay extra attention to alcohol consumption by minors. Large events are always monitored, however on regular club evenings, monitoring usually only occurs if there are reports (from surrounding residents, for example).
statements.pro: The regulations exist to protect young people, and should be enforced. Strict monitoring helps prevent alcohol consumption among under-18s. Enforcement is necessary in cases of dangerous behaviour, such as alcohol poisoning.
statements.con: Clubs are run by volunteers, and they should not be burdened with more monitoring. The municipality should give clubs support and trust, not punish them. Enforcement should only take place if there are serious problems or infringements.

statements.title: People should perform community service in return for their benefit payments.

titleParts.glossary: This can include cleaning public spaces, or providing companionship and support to senior citizens.

titleParts.text: in return for their benefit payments.

statements.moreInfo: People on welfare benefits receive money from the municipality because they have little or no income of their own. They often also have obligations, such as looking for work or attending support sessions. Some municipalities ask welfare recipients to do community service. This is work that is beneficial to society, such as helping in a community centre, plant maintenance, or helping to support community activities. This work is usually not paid. The municipality could decide to make this kind of work mandatory for welfare recipients.
statements.pro: People who are able to work should give something back to the community. Community service provides structure and rhythm. Useful work helps people move into paid work.
statements.con: Not everybody on welfare is in a position to do community service. Mandatory work without pay is not a good idea. Voluntary participation with support works better than imposing obligations.

statements.title: The municipality should increase the citizen budget. The citizen budget gives Maastricht residents a joint say in how some municipal funds are spent.
statements.moreInfo: Every year, the municipality of Maastricht draws up a budget, setting out how much money the municipality receives and what it will be spent on. In a citizen budget, residents themselves decide how some of the municipal funds are spent. The municipality sets the amount, and residents decide together on what it should be spent on.Maastricht already has a citizen budget. Residents can submit proposals, and decide together on which ideas should be implemented. In 2025, the citizen budget totalled €750,000.
statements.pro: Residents know best what they need in their city or neighbourhood. A citizen budget gives residents real control over municipal funds. Joint decisions on public spending create more confidence in government.
statements.con: The current citizen budget is already high enough. The citizen budget mainly goes toward people with lots of time and a large network. Additional funds could be better spent directly on neighbourhoods and clubs/associations.

statements.title: The municipality should stop the growth of student housing in residential areas.
statements.moreInfo: Maastricht is home to many students. Some live in ordinary residential areas, in ‘student houses.’ Maastricht’s housing policy contains regulations for student accommodation, some of which apply to splitting up houses into separate rooms for rent.
statements.pro: Too many student houses create disruption in residential areas. Residential neighbourhoods need to remain liveable for local residents. Campuses are a better place for students than residential neighbourhoods.
statements.con: A complete ban is too strict, and not necessary. Students are also residents, and deserve to be part of the city. An effective mix of housing in the city is a better solution than strict separation.

statements.title: The municipality should ensure that cultural free spaces, such as the Landbouwbelang, can continue to exist in the city.

titleParts.glossary: Places where artists and creators can work and experiment freely.

titleParts.text: , such as the Landbouwbelang, can continue to exist in the city.

statements.moreInfo: Cultural free spaces are places where people practice culture together, such as music and art. They often do so voluntarily and without making a profit. These locations provide opportunities for the growth of new ideas, and for people who cannot find a place anywhere else. The municipality can help by providing locations, or modifying legislation. The municipality decides whether, and how, it wishes to support these spaces.
statements.pro: Cultural free spaces create a lively city. They provide space for creativity and new ideas. They are important to facilitate interaction between different types of people.
statements.con: The municipality is not responsible for funding cultural free spaces. Free spaces should not provide unfair competition for businesses. The same rules should apply everywhere, including cultural free spaces.

statements.title: The municipality should encourage housing associations to allow social housing tenants to buy their homes.

titleParts.glossary: Non-profit organisations that focus on the construction, management, and lease of affordable housing.

titleParts.text: to allow social housing tenants to buy their homes.

statements.moreInfo: Rental social housing is a type of housing for people on low incomes. Some social rental tenants want to purchase their homes, which is often possible under the Dutch Home Ownership (Promotion) Act. This means that tenants can purchase their homes if permitted by the lessor (e.g. a housing association).
statements.pro: Buying a home gives tenants greater security.It increases their engagement with the neighbourhood.The revenue can be used to build more social rentals.
statements.con: Social housing stock is already too limited.Selling social housing will make waiting lists even longer.Social rentals are meant for people who cannot afford to buy their own home.

statements.title: The municipality should make free tampons and pads available at more locations.
statements.moreInfo: Menstrual products are essentials for women and girls during their period. Not everybody can easily afford or obtain these products. The municipality of Maastricht already has distribution points for menstrual products, such as pads and tampons. These points are currently managed by the Poverty Fund (Armoedefonds), an organisation that helps people with limited means. The municipality could decide to distribute tampons and pads in more locations, such as public buildings, sports locations, or schools.
statements.pro: Menstrual products are essentials, and should be available to everyone. Not everybody can easily afford tampons or pads. This way, girls and women will not need to stay at home during their period.
statements.con: Free menstrual products are already available from many locations. It is not the municipality’s job to provide these products. Free products will create additional expenses for the municipality.

shootoutStatements.title: The municipality should not expand paid parking in the outer districts.
